> [View attachment 25751](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/25751/)[View attachment 25752](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/25752/)

For a 3 gram serving of the 50/50 mix, only 1.5 grams is chlorella, so the spermidine dose would be minimal, even if the pyrenoidosa is the right strain. Where I buy it from the regular chlorella is pyrenoidosa, but the chlorella promoting the spermidine content I don't know which strain it is, but as they are the same price I haven't gone into it further, I just buy the one where the spermidine is measured.

Its just a musing on my part  but if spermidine synthase is disrupted by the virus or the spike proteins (inc from jabs), then putrescine is less likely to be converted to spermidine, so during infection or dealing with long covid, temporarily cutting down on putrescine from dietary intake and by doing IF and adding a source of spermidine, if it has the potential of salvaging autophagy to help clear the spike proteins, for me its worth a go, particularly because of the other nutrients. But how I do it and how much to take is n=1, particularly because of my age. I calculated roughly my intake from the summer seasonal epi paleo, and I can adjust the dose as my diet changes over the winter. But if I became infected or was suffering from long covid, I'd be changing things up, particularly as the quantum yield is dropping like a stone.

 wiki.plantpills.co.uk

ADMIT IT, YOU DON’T LIKE THE COLD, LIFELESS LIGHT THAT EMITS FROM THESE BULBS, BUT WHAT YOU DON’T KNOW MAY HURT YOU! Better to vie for ...

 thropaynutrition.blogspot.com

# Thread: UK Bottled Water
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mackerelx10 — 2023-10-17T22:36:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-327370>

Hey,

I live in the South West of England. I've been avoiding drinking tap water for about a year now, mostly drinking Highland Spring instead. Main reasons being it's from a high latitude, hasn't travelled too far and is available in enclosed 10L boxes so less exposure to light in transit etc. Anyway, I've just read on an older thread that someone had it tested for heavy metals and it was high in barium, so thinking time to switch. Also read that Evian has the lowest level of deuterium, at least out of the ones I saw that are easily available in the UK.

Another I've drank a fair bit is Brecon Carreg which is even more local to me but sourced at a lower latitude. I think the boxed Highland Spring was what swayed my decision til now but I'm sure there's more important things to consider. I'm fairly new to this stuff but picking things up as I go, just trying to figure out the best options for me right now (living in a city on a tight budget). Any opinions or useful tips would be greatly appreciated!

Blessings

### Sue-UK — 2023-10-19T10:09:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-327416>

Hi

I was living in the South West til a couple of years ago, now in the South East.

Fiddling around with a few ppm deuterium between different UK bottled waters isn't one of my criteria, particularly on a tight budget. Avoiding deuterium loaded crap food or supplements is higher on my list as is improving the endogenous deuterium depleting pathways. I don't use one brand of water, at the moment I'm using 3 - I call it going to a different watering hole.  One is a general UK bottled spring water - "not tap water" brand, even that varies depending on which supermarket I use, and what's on offer, the other 2 are for therapeutic purposes, based on my age and n=1.

### [25 HydroHealth (24 bottles x 500ml Box) $190 incl.S&H-no PO box- Choos](https://25-ddw-hydrohealth.myshopify.com/products/25-hydrohealth-24pcs-x-500ml-box)

Best Price: $190 for one case, 24 bottles, 16.90 fl. oz. (500 ML) each, $7.90 per bottle, including S&H. Subscribe and save an additional 10%($19). GET ONE BOX MONTHLY FOR ONLY $171 ($7.12 per bottle)- SHIPPING INCLUDED Please also review our subscription policy. Visit "About Subscriptions" No...

25-ddw-hydrohealth.myshopify.com

I buy my DDW water from the place above.
They sell variety 25, 12, and 50ppm D/H
but the 25ppm works the best for me.
I drink on average 125ppm water.
That is a 4 half liter bottles of RO water and one half liter 25ppm
\=(1\*25+4\*150)/5=125
My tests and other info are on this thread:

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-make-ddw-do-you-buy-this-50ppm.20927/page-8#post-239806](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-make-ddw-do-you-buy-this-50ppm.20927/page-8#post-239806)

I know that that water works because **for three years I have been drinking exclusively** that 25ppm water and numerous times tested how it works for me by testing.
Before I started drinking all that I made a test to see my starting situation.
I tested condensate of my breath and saliva.
They were much higher on the beginning at progresively lower in deuterium latter on.
I have posted my results on this board.
Working with the outfit that initially had dr Boros helping them, I have found that:

Sue what are the three you buy and also are they low in minerals for putting in the freezer to drink from regarding the deuterated water?

### Sue-UK — 2023-10-23T09:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-327489>

> [Josie Thomson said:](/goto/post?id=327480)
>
> Sue what are the three you buy and also are they low in minerals for putting in the freezer to drink from regarding the deuterated water?

I have done that method before, but don't have the freezer space now, and I don't think the amount depleted makes it worth it. I use a silicon rich mineral water to deplete aluminum, which is implicated in DH and my maternal mitochondrial DNA risks, heart disease and dementia, and Jack has talked about aluminum's reflecting properties. So getting aluminum out has the potential to improve endogenous light signalling etc. The other one I use is an oxygenated water mentioned by Que Collins (who was one of the co-founders of the Centre of Deuterium Depletion) who said in an interview (interview and transcript at [https://melanieavalon.com/water/](https://melanieavalon.com/water/))

_"Then, we use water as a way to get you there. But here's what I think, is what I'm really happy about, is using this new Kure oxygenated water, it's very, very inexpensive. It's only about $2 a bottle, so it's the same price as a bottle of Fiji water, Perrier water, whatever, but you're able to use that.

If you think about the deuterium-depleted water, it is what I like to call passive depletion. In other words, you're diluting-- you're trying to get everything lower and in time, because it's lower, it's going to make you better. With Kure oxygenated water, it’s just the opposite. It's active. It's like having a fighter plane. What it's going to do, you're going to drink it, these ultra-fine bubbles are going to float through, and we have pictures and everything of how it works. It is pretty amazing. It's going to flow to your organs, get into your mitochondria, and then bind a hydrogen deuterium, and remove it from the system. **What's nice is oxygen has an affinity about six times or seven times the amount of affinity for deuterium than it does for hydrogen. It's going to bind to deuterium first,** that's one of the reasons that we breathe. Believe it or not, remind me to talk about that. But oxygen binds deuterium to make heavy water, and it's heavy, because remember deuterium is twice as heavy, so it's physically heavy, and our body has all these systems built in to specifically get rid of this heavy water. "_

I think there's a possibility that they also work synergistically in removing aluminum and deuterium.

When I get a drink of water, I mix 400ml of cold silicon rich water (currently Volvic as its easily available in the supermarkets) with 100ml of cold oxygenated water and drink it fairly quickly, and use the "not tap water" for anything that is going to be heated such as hot drinks or cooking. The oxygenated water is spring water from Cornwall infused with oxygen, and that's the only one I get delivered. Its currently marketed for sports recovery, where someone might drink a whole bottle after a workout, but I'm not sure about that for deuterium depletion for my n=1. The DD centre are conducting a trial on its use in covid, and says
_"This could possibly be happening since Kure delivers 100 times more oxygen than regular water in tiny, stable, ultrafine bubbles."_ which is far more oxygen than the other oxygenated water research I've read**,** which is why I cut it to the ratio I use, because I'm not dealing with cancer etc or anything covid related.

([https://www.ddcenters.com/kure-covid-study2/](https://www.ddcenters.com/kure-covid-study2/))

At £24.99 for 24 x 500ml bottles and £1 for 1 1/2 liters of volvic its more affordable than DDW.

Thanks A

I've continued using the same waters since my post in October 23. I've accepted that although what I buy is in plastic bottles, the advantages of aluminium removal and moderate deuterium depletion and avoiding tap water outweighs the negatives for my n=1.

Although I'm still using Kure, I am less picky about measuring it out with 400ml of water and 100ml Kure, mainly just use it intuitively.

The way I look at it is that if all the deuterium in my body suddenly vanished, I'd probably be a puddle on the floor - so my messing about with excessive deuterium depletion isn't without risk. Deuterium in the wrong place such as the mitochondria is another issue, which is one reason I use Kure. The other reason I use Kure is a pic Jack put in my journal when I mentioned the oxygenated water hack ..

[https://static.webshopapp.com/shops...08/pakdaman-studies-oxygen-enriched-water.pdf](https://static.webshopapp.com/shops/035143/files/056059208/pakdaman-studies-oxygen-enriched-water.pdf)
Oxygen enriched water and Oral Oxygen Therapy (OOT)

Fig.2: A reduction of O2 content in the blood is observed with increasing age.

(This could also affect deuterium depletion).

Herrera Human Photosynthesis book says that we begin to lose our capacity to split and reform the water molecule (i.e. a role of melanin) at around 26, losing function at an approx rate of 10 per cent with each decade after mid twenties, and it goes into free fall when we reach our 50s.

Doug Wallace heteroplasmy worsening 10 per cent per decade .....

So there's the deuterium angle, but my decision to continue using it is also based on oxygen and my being in my mid sixties .... Its difficult to quantify the effects of one water when its in a combination with everything else I do, but if it mitigates some of the deuterium/less blood O2 risks because of my age its worth it for me.

Sue, what are your thoughts on H2 O2 diluted in water.. I've been trying it out in our small essential oil diffuser, it doesn't feel like it's doing much.... Thinking it helps with a similar effect to what your involved in here... but I don't know about the overall benefits this way tbh.

### Sue-UK — 2024-07-25T17:46:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-337563>

> [Josie Thomson said:](/goto/post?id=337558)
>
> Sue, what are your thoughts on H2 O2 diluted in water.. I've been trying it out in our small essential oil diffuser, it doesn't feel like it's doing much.... Thinking it helps with a similar effect to what your involved in here... but I don't know about the overall benefits this way tbh.

I don't think the effects of hydrogen peroxide is similar to what I'm hacking.  Although most of what I've seen is warning against the risks from people using it in nebulizers, rather than diffusing it into the air from an essential oil diffuser, hydrogen peroxide via the lungs is not something I'd play with on my own without really understanding what it was doing. For example some bacteria such as _Streptococcus pneumoniae_ produce hydrogen peroxide.

_"Here, we demonstrated that S. pn-secreted H2O2 caused mitochondrial damage and severe histopathological damage in mouse lung tissue. Additionally, S. pn-secreted H2O2 caused not only oxidative damage to mitochondrial deoxyribonucleic acid (mtDNA),. but also a reduction in the mtDNA content in alveolar epithelia cells. "_

### [Frontiers | Mitochondrial DNA Leakage Caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae Hydrogen Peroxide Promotes Type I IFN Expression in Lung Cells](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2019.00630/full)

Streptococcus pneumoniae (S. pn), the bacterial pathogen responsible for invasive pneumococcal diseases, is capable of producing substantial amounts of hydro...

 www.frontiersin.org

Although the paper is about mice lung tissue, and what the experiment was may make a difference blah blah, it makes me cautious, although a small essential oil diffuser in a room is possibly not doing much depending on the overall air volume etc.

> ### [Frontiers | Mitochondrial DNA Leakage Caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae Hydrogen Peroxide Promotes Type I IFN Expression in Lung Cells](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2019.00630/full)
>
> Streptococcus pneumoniae (S. pn), the bacterial pathogen responsible for invasive pneumococcal diseases, is capable of producing substantial amounts of hydro...
>
>  www.frontiersin.org
>
>
> Although the paper is about mice lung tissue, and what the experiment was may make a difference blah blah, it makes me cautious, although a small essential oil diffuser in a room is possibly not doing much depending on the overall air volume etc.

Thanks Sue.

### Annelieuk — 2024-07-25T20:15:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-337576>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=337514)
>
> I've continued using the same waters since my post in October 23. I've accepted that although what I buy is in plastic bottles, the advantages of aluminium removal and moderate deuterium depletion and avoiding tap water outweighs the negatives for my n=1.
>
> Although I'm still using Kure, I am less picky about measuring it out with 400ml of water and 100ml Kure, mainly just use it intuitively.
>
> The way I look at it is that if all the deuterium in my body suddenly vanished, I'd probably be a puddle on the floor - so my messing about with excessive deuterium depletion isn't without risk. Deuterium in the wrong place such as the mitochondria is another issue, which is one reason I use Kure. The other reason I use Kure is a pic Jack put in my journal when I mentioned the oxygenated water hack ..
>
>
>
>
> [https://static.webshopapp.com/shops...08/pakdaman-studies-oxygen-enriched-water.pdf](https://static.webshopapp.com/shops/035143/files/056059208/pakdaman-studies-oxygen-enriched-water.pdf)
> Oxygen enriched water and Oral Oxygen Therapy (OOT)
>
> Fig.2: A reduction of O2 content in the blood is observed with increasing age.
>
> (This could also affect deuterium depletion).
>
> Herrera Human Photosynthesis book says that we begin to lose our capacity to split and reform the water molecule (i.e. a role of melanin) at around 26, losing function at an approx rate of 10 per cent with each decade after mid twenties, and it goes into free fall when we reach our 50s.
>
> Doug Wallace heteroplasmy worsening 10 per cent per decade .....
>
> So there's the deuterium angle, but my decision to continue using it is also based on oxygen and my being in my mid sixties .... Its difficult to quantify the effects of one water when its in a combination with everything else I do, but if it mitigates some of the deuterium/less blood O2 risks because of my age its worth it for me.

very interesting! Thanks Sue! Where are you based in the U.K?

### Annelieuk — 2024-07-26T10:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-337591>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=327416)
>
> Hi
>
> I was living in the South West til a couple of years ago, now in the South East.
>
> Fiddling around with a few ppm deuterium between different UK bottled waters isn't one of my criteria, particularly on a tight budget. Avoiding deuterium loaded crap food or supplements is higher on my list as is improving the endogenous deuterium depleting pathways. I don't use one brand of water, at the moment I'm using 3 - I call it going to a different watering hole.  One is a general UK bottled spring water - "not tap water" brand, even that varies depending on which supermarket I use, and what's on offer, the other 2 are for therapeutic purposes, based on my age and n=1.

Hey Sue, when it comes to the deuterium content of supplements, is there any rule of thumb of which ones are worse? I am assuming ones with fillers are more of the problem? Also, do you know if taking collagen and gelatin are high deuterium as know that there is often where the body is naturally higher in deuterium. Many thanks

### Sue-UK — 2024-07-26T17:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-337599>

> [Annelieuk said:](/goto/post?id=337591)
>
> Hey Sue, when it comes to the deuterium content of supplements, is there any rule of thumb of which ones are worse? I am assuming ones with fillers are more of the problem? Also, do you know if taking collagen and gelatin are high deuterium as know that there is often where the body is naturally higher in deuterium. Many thanks

It gets too complicated for me .... There's fillers, the manufacturing process, what an animal ate for an animal derived product etc, and added to Jack's SUNplement not supplement, its something I try to avoid. But if I chose to take a supplement, its the context again ....from a 30,000 ft view would some deuterium in a supplement be such an issue in an otherwise low deuterium intake? That might even vary seasonally. My bigger issue is that if a capsule or tablet is swallowed with water, the gut and the body gets what it gets, whether it can deal with it or not, without the signalling from the mouth I'd get from a whole real food. I crave something until I don't. At the moment I use spirulina and chlorella, which are grown as crops, and IP6 gold which I get in powder form to mix in water and a dash of ACV rather than capsules. I can taste these things, which I think can give advance notice of what is going down the chute, and stop if its not right.

### Espresso — 2023-10-24T14:58:19+0300

Fitting heat sinks with double sided tape pretty misses the point. In order to get the heat out, one would need to have direct contact with the heat source. Might as well just use blocks of wood to stiffen up the panel.

### RitaC — 2023-10-28T17:11:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-light.28269/post-327608>

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=327472)
>
> They both look nice, I have not used either but from a practical point of view the larger coverage area of the Inferno for daily maintenance/treatment would to me appear to be a better approach. I have two 850nm NIR lights I use that give good coverage and results I am happy with. NIR security lights are 850nm, I payed less than $200 for the two lights I have and I added a UVA light.

What UVA light did you add, why, and how do you use it?

### Anthony Smith — 2023-10-29T23:01:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-light.28269/post-327649>

> [RitaC said:](/goto/post?id=327608)
>
> What UVA light did you add, why, and how do you use it?

I got the one Jack uses, it is offered in 30Watt or 60Watt I ordered the 60Watt but when it came it says 100Watt on the back. I later found out Jack’s is the 30AMP so I use mine a few feet away. This light has no ground and the electric field from it is significant so I recommend grounding the light. I use 2 850nm NIR lights first especially when indoors looking at a screen and add the UVA light during the day. This set up is behind and above my screen. Each NIR light is equivalent to a 500Watt quartz halogen work light, but being NIR all you see is a gentle glow.

I like the 2 NIR light set up because when using for healing I can turn them toward each other and treat front and back at the same time - shoulder, knee etc. I would be much closer and leave the UVA light off when doing that. I will use the NIR by itself BUT never use the UVA without the NIR. Others may disagree but this what I have and do, it works for me for my wife and I for pain relief and healing.

Total set up: 2 NIR lights, UVA light and stand (from HomeDepot) about $250.

### Phil — 2023-11-07T23:14:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-light.28269/post-328015>

> [Espresso said:](/goto/post?id=327510)
>
> Fitting heat sinks with double sided tape pretty misses the point. In order to get the heat out, one would need to have direct contact with the heat source. Might as well just use blocks of wood to stiffen up the panel.

I used thermal adhesive tape, I could have used thermal adhesive glue. The future models of the improve panel will have heat sinks built in for added support and to dissipate some of the heat. This panel is pretty low intensity so heat sinks are not really needed. Just using blocks of wood is actually a good idea

### Espresso — 2023-11-08T10:16:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-light.28269/post-328021>

> [Phil said:](/goto/post?id=328015)

This panel is pretty low intensity so heat sinks are not really needed. Just using blocks of wood is actually a good idea

Well, you have taught me something. I didn't know that one could get thermal adhesive tape. Thanks.

# Thread: Raw eggs as a base for Leptin Reset
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-eggs-as-a-base-for-leptin-reset.28270/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Elkim — 2023-10-22T11:00:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-eggs-as-a-base-for-leptin-reset.28270/post-327477>

I'm not a much breakfast person since forever and always had issues to eat large breakfasts. When it comes to animal products alone without a side dish, it's even worse. 3-4 scrambled eggs is max. I can do before a switch flips in my head and I can't swallow it anymore a start retching - and that's only if I have some carbs like sourdough bread. Same thing with meat or fresh cheese without carbs to rotate between bites. Forcing it causes a lot of stress. I suspect it might be a eating disorder caused by the Leptin resistance.

However I found I could hack it with just raw eggs blended a bit and drink full of glass it. The rest of the 50g of protein I could probably finish with bacon and fresh cheese or pecorino.

I can't think reason why would raw eggs be bad choice but asking anyway. Those would of course be pasture-raised eggs from happy hens. It also seems like the least hustle option as it take few minutes to prepare and I can take it outside to A.M. sunlight.

Thanks

### Dr Rohen Kapur — 2023-10-22T11:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-eggs-as-a-base-for-leptin-reset.28270/post-327479>

> [Elkim said:](/goto/post?id=327477)
>
> I'm not a much breakfast person since forever and always had issues to eat large breakfasts. When it comes to animal products alone without a side dish, it's even worse. 3-4 scrambled eggs is max. I can do before a switch flips in my head and I can't swallow it anymore a start retching - and that's only if I have some carbs like sourdough bread. Same thing with meat or fresh cheese without carbs to rotate between bites. Forcing it causes a lot of stress. I suspect it might be a eating disorder caused by the Leptin resistance.
>
> However I found I could hack it with just raw eggs blended a bit and drink full of glass it. The rest of the 50g of protein I could probably finish with bacon and fresh cheese or pecorino.
>
> I can't think reason why would raw eggs be bad choice but asking anyway. Those would of course be pasture-raised eggs from happy hens. It also seems like the least hustle option as it take few minutes to prepare and I can take it outside to A.M. sunlight.
>
> Thanks

Back when I was doing keto and I was late for dinner, I went into a convenience store and purchased six organic eggs ( it was about 8.30pm and I was hungry )

I then went out side the store and bit the tops off, and drank them down all six of them. Half an hour later I felt replenished.

I would say in my opinion that there's nothing wrong with raw eggs from a recognised source

### JanSz — 2023-10-22T23:16:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-eggs-as-a-base-for-leptin-reset.28270/post-327483>

> [Dr Rohen Kapur said:](/goto/post?id=327479)
>
> I would say in my opinion that there's nothing wrong with raw eggs from a recognised source

I worked in the city, visiting my parent who had little farm in the sticks.
On the way home I would go with the suitcase full of eggs.
No refrigeration, stored in the place that was hot, near roof of the home.
Eggs were sometimes waiting to be eaten for 2 months or more.
I drank them raw.
By the end, 1/4 of the egg was missed, evaporated.
Newer got sick via eating anything, not only raw eggs.

I still think that storing eggs in refrigerator is a waste of refrigerator's space.
...

### Anthony Smith — 2023-10-23T01:40:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-eggs-as-a-base-for-leptin-reset.28270/post-327485>

Eggs fresh from a farm and NOT bleached can be stored out of the fridge just like you did - BUT store bought eggs have been washed in bleach and have lost their natural protective coating and MUST be refrigerated.

### Highlander999 — 2023-11-06T08:02:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-eggs-as-a-base-for-leptin-reset.28270/post-327904>

I read somewhere that raw eggs are fine, the biggest cause for any issue (contaminant) is from the shell; so I guess avoid dropping the shell into the mix - I like my raw eggs in a glass with a small amount of milk. As mentioned, a nice quick meal!

# Thread: Robert O. Becker's: "The Body Electric"and a cancer quote...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/robert-o-beckers-the-body-electric-and-a-cancer-quote.28276/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RisleyDC — 2023-10-25T02:52:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/robert-o-beckers-the-body-electric-and-a-cancer-quote.28276/post-327525>

Halfway through Beckers book and found a provocative cancer theory by G. Andres of Switzerland (pg. 216). "A normal cell becomes cancerous by dedifferentiation. This change is not dangerous per se, according to Andres, but, because it occurs in a postfetal animal, **the controls that would normally hold these neo-embryonic cells in check aren't working."**

"Those animals that regenerate best are least susceptible to cancer. In general, as complexity increases up the evolutionary ladder from worms to humans, regeneration decreases, and cancer becomes more common. (Salamanders) have tremendous regenerative abilities and almost no cancer. Even to give them tumors in the laboratory requires much effort."

When the controls of light are not present or are present in the wrong wavelengths... This brings new insight to comments made about metastasis as cells "looking for light" by Dr. Kruse

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2023-10-30T21:54:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/robert-o-beckers-the-body-electric-and-a-cancer-quote.28276/post-327681>

Interesting. Thanks for sharing!

### Johan Lindøm — 2023-10-31T08:50:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/robert-o-beckers-the-body-electric-and-a-cancer-quote.28276/post-327710>

Yep, I noticed this too on my second run through the book. Fits in well with what jack tells us. Amazing that more people don't understand what this man accomplished.

# Thread: Other food options for chitin, iodine, and carotenoids

**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions

### coho3131 — 2023-10-30T01:42:05+0200

### JanSz — 2023-10-30T02:39:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/other-food-options-for-chitin-iodine-and-carotenoids.28281/post-327653>

**Food options for chitin, iodine, and carotenoids

Chitin:** Chitin is a type of fiber found in the shells of crustaceans such as shrimp, crab, and lobster.
It is also present in the cell walls of fungi.
Some food items that contain chitin are shrimp, crab, lobster, mushrooms, and yeast.

**Iodine:** Iodine is an essential mineral that is required for the production of thyroid hormones.
Some food items that are rich in iodine are seaweed, cod, yogurt, milk, and eggs.

**Carotenoids:** Carotenoids are organic pigments that are found in plants, algae, fungi, and bacteria.
They are believed to be linked to a lower risk of chronic disease and cardiovascular illness and could even potentially help prevent or fight some cancers.
Some food items that contain carotenoids are:
Carrots are an excellent source of carotenoids.
Just half a cup of cooked carrots contains 6499 mcg of β-carotene and 2945 mcg of α-carotene.
Spinach: Spinach is another great source of carotenoids.
Half a cup of boiled spinach contains 6875 mcg of β-carotene and 14906 mcg of lutein + zeaxanthin.
Kale: Kale is a cruciferous vegetable that is packed with nutrients.
Half a cup of boiled kale contains 5735 mcg of β-carotene and 12803 mcg of lutein + zeaxanthin.
Pumpkin: A single cup serving of pumpkin, boiled and mashed, has 6652 mcg of α-carotene, 5135 mcg of β-carotene, and 2484 mcg of lutein + zeaxanthin.

I hope this helps!
/

### JanSz — 2023-10-30T02:45:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/other-food-options-for-chitin-iodine-and-carotenoids.28281/post-327655>

**I’m sorry to hear that you cannot eat shellfish**. Here are some other sources of chitin:
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

-   [**Mushrooms**: Chitin is present in the cell walls of fungi, including mushrooms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chitin).

-   **Insects**: Insects are another source of chitin. [Some insects that contain chitin are **crickets**, **grasshoppers**, and **beetles**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chitin).
-   **Algae**: Some species of algae contain chitin. [For example, the cell walls of diatoms, which are a type of algae, contain chitin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chitin).
-   [**Fungal mycelium waste sources**: Chitin can also be derived from fungal mycelium waste sources](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4614-1247-2_4).

I hope this helps!
[](https://www.mushroomrevival.com/blogs/blog/chitin-an-important-component-in-mushrooms)

# Thread: Magnetico Sleep Pad - EMF Shielding

### Mike117 — 2023-10-31T14:24:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetico-sleep-pad-emf-shielding.28286/post-327714>

Hi Jack,

When purchasing an Magnetico Sleep Pad, is it necessary first to shield the bedroom completely from EMF since the pad contains metal? Or is the benefits from the Magnetico going to offset any negative effects from EMF in the environment?

Thanks!

### Anthony Smith — 2023-10-31T18:46:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetico-sleep-pad-emf-shielding.28286/post-327725>

I know you asked Jack but I went to the Magnetico web site and in the Q&A they say no electronic devices should be close to the pad. Since any interference comes from the electric and magnetic fields put out by these devices and the wires in the wall next to or floor under the bed put out 60Hz AC field 6 - 8 FEET in all directions, it might be a good idea to turn off any circuits in and around the bedroom, which EVERYONE should do anyway, Magnetico or not. With electric and magnetic fields it is much easier to just turn off the source rather than shield. Measure and know exactly what nnEMF you have - [www.LiveEMFSafe.com/kruse](http://www.LiveEMFSafe.com/kruse)

From the Magnetico web site: “As per our FAQ, if you choose to use any electronic devices near or on the Sleep Pad, you do so at your own risk. Magnetic fields can interact with electronic devices, and we cannot guarantee no temporary or permanent damage won’t happen with your device. Dr. Bonlie also does not suggest using other devices such as ground pads/sheets, PEMF devices, or or other electronics near you while you sleep as these can interfere with the function of the Sleep Pad.”

The Magnetico is going to offset nnEMF ,however to receive maximum advantage with the Pad it would be beneficial to increase your Seafood if you are receiving negative effects from the pad when first sleeping on it as some members of this forum have experienced.

# Thread: antenna search map
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antenna-search-map.28298/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### coho3131 — 2023-11-06T15:20:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antenna-search-map.28298/post-327910>

Do the towers generally transmit more nnEMF than the antennas?

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-06T18:03:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antenna-search-map.28298/post-327922>

Just different frequencies. I understand towers to be mainly phone 3G, 4G & (maybe) 5G. You will see a phone company name next to many. Antennas are mainly radio: police, fire, taxi, transport companies, radio stations etc. You will see the name of the company, local council etc next to many of these. Both distance and power need to be considered. Small towers put out less that big towers.

The Sutro Tower in San Francisco is huge and has EVERYTHING on it, I’ve read the the incidence of cancer within 2 miles of the Sutro Tower is 5X the normal and 2X as far as 5 miles out. Years ago (3G days) I read the incidence of cancer in a small town in Europe with one “small” cell tower was 2x within 400 meters (1,200 feet) but normal beyond that.

### coho3131 — 2023-11-06T22:17:03+0200

Both distance and power need to be considered. Small towers put out less that big towers.

# Thread: Thiamine for someone following a semi-SAD
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TaylorF — 2023-11-06T16:01:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/post-327912>

Hello,

I'd like to start my mother on a regiment of magnesium and thiamine. She is pre-diabetic (doctor recommended trying Wegovy), only about 25-30 lbs overweight, she has issues with her nervous system, neck, and vagus nerve trouble (vasovagal symptoms).

I'm working on getting her to turn off the wifi and leave her phone off in the kitchen at night, and have her wearing blue blockers after sunset. She eats a fairly standard diet, protein bars or egg bites for breakfast, turkey, cheese, snacks with lunch, and usually shrimp and vegetables or a salad for dinner, she also likes sugar, usually in the form of hot cocoa or an organic cookie brand.

I'm wondering what dose of thiamine and magnesium I should have her start taking, especially since she takes vitamin d with k2 and I've gotten her levels up to 60, but that's mostly through supplements.

Thanks for any and all tips.

Taylor

### JanSz — 2023-11-06T16:40:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/post-327915>

pre-diabetic= high fasting insulin
Work on her to eventually arrive at eating only within 4-6 hours and during daylight.
Think also about modifying intake of major protein sources from meat to vegetables,
eating less meats much more legumes.
One major reason farmed (organic or not) animals, birds and fish contains insane amounts of linoleic acid.

Get her a scale that show weight and body fat.
Tell her to go on that scale and check it, every time she wants to eat.
\--------
vitamin d with k2
Make sure that k2 contains Menaquinone-7 or MK7
also add Serrapeptase, or nattokinase or lumbrokinase.
Serrapeptase is least expensive

Vit D (and A) are better when eating
Belveder Premium Wild Cod Liver in Own Oil
buy a lot of it at one time, on amazon, they will cost same as in store or less but will be much fresher.

///////////////
If you live in high population density, and not able to relocate:
do not worry about minute details of nnEMF, spend your energy on other things.
...

Very important question.
Do not use any indicators or evaluators.
Test them directly and draw your own conclusions.
For myself I use this test:

Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum

Then read results and calculate this relation:
LA/ALA

LA-Linoleic Acid (Omega-6)
ALA-Alpha Linolenic Acid (Omega-3)

# Thread: Wall During a Marathon
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-during-a-marathon.28302/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-11-07T17:03:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-during-a-marathon.28302/post-327979>

### How to Calculate and Predict When You’ll Hit the Wall During a Marathon​

### [How to Calculate and Predict When You’ll Hit the Wall During a Marathon - Runners Connect](https://runnersconnect.net/marathon-hitting-the-wall/)

"Hitting the marathon wall" is a much-feared and much-discussed phenomenon in marathoning. Runners speak ominously about a sudden wave of fatigue that sets in at about 20 miles into a marathon. At this point, they say, the race is half over. Contending with the wall is a problem whether you're...

 runnersconnect.net

\-----------

#### John Davis​

“_Hitting the marathon wall_” is a much-feared and much-discussed phenomenon in marathoning. Runners speak ominously about a sudden wave of fatigue that sets in at about 20 miles into a marathon. At this point, they say, the race is half over.
Contending with the wall is a problem whether you’re an elite runner or recreational jogger.
Thanks to research by exercise physiologists, we know that runners “hit the wall” because they run out of carbohydrates stored in their body and have to suddenly shift to burning mostly fat to keep them going ([recap on the science of bonking and glycogen depletion](https://runnersconnect.net/running-training-articles/cience-of-bonking-and-glycogen-depletion/))
Understanding where the wall comes from is one thing, but predicting it and determining how to overcome it is a bit more challenging.
Today, we’ll take a look at the efforts of one researcher to develop a mathematical model which can predict whether you’ll hit the wall in your next marathon, and where exactly you’ll hit it.

## Rapoport’s Energy Consumption Model​

The researcher, Benjamin Rapoport, is an MD-PhD candidate at Harvard and MIT. In 2010, he published a paper which described his model for tracking the energy consumption of a marathon runner.
The premise of Rapoport’s model is fairly simple: it takes a certain amount of energy per mile and per pound of body weight to move a runner from point A to point B. Converting to metric, this amount is conveniently about one calorie (as in food calories) per kilogram of body mass per kilometer of running.
**This would mean a 150-pound runner requires around 110 calories per mile, regardless of pace.**
But, there are also two more variables to consider above simply weight and distance:

### Energy expenditure from fat​

In Rapoport’s model, the per-mile energy expenditure of runners doesn’t vary based on the speed they run, but _where_ they get their energy from does.
Though runners mostly think about carbohydrates as their principle energy source, a significant portion of your energy expenditure while running a marathon comes from fat.
At easy paces, you burn more fat; as you run faster, your body begins to rely more heavily on carbohydrates.
So, on a typical easy run,
**you might be burning 60% carbs and 40% fat.**
But in a 5k race,
**well over 90% of your energy is going to be coming from carbs**
—you just can’t burn fat quickly enough to produce enough energy to [run 5k pace](https://runnersconnect.net/5k-and-10k-training/).
Keep in mind that _**hitting the wall**_ **happens when you run out of** _**carbs**_ (your fat supply is effectively unlimited), so to predict if and when this will happen, we need to know **how fast** you’re running.

### Carbohydrates​

The final variable in the mathematical model estimates how much energy you have stored up in your body in the form of carbohydrates.
There are two sources for your body to access carbs when you run: **carbs stored in your liver, and carbs stored in your leg muscles.**
According to Rapoport, the size and energy density of the liver are tightly regulated by the body, so this amount is only dependent on your body mass (as the liver makes up about 2.5% of your weight). The energy stored in your leg muscles also depends on your body mass, but also the size of your legs and the density of carbohydrates stored in them.

## The Equation​

Rapoport provides a range of typical values for each of these: for most people, leg muscles make up about 21% of their body mass, and a typical runner stores about 36 calories worth of carbs per pound of muscle.
By doing some simple algebra, we can create an equation to calculate how far you can run before you expend all of your carbohydrate energy and hence hit the wall.
Once you’ve used up your energy stores in your liver and your legs, you can go no further—at least, not without a massive shift in carbohydrate consumption and likely a massive drop in speed as well.

## Calculating When You Will Hit The Wall​

Rapoport’s model is best displayed as a graph, reproduced below.
The only inputs you need to figure out are the relative speed you intend to run your marathon at, expressed as a percentage of your VO2 max, and the amount of carbs stored up in your leg muscles. Surprisingly, body weight doesn’t factor into the final result: although heavier runners require more energy to cover a given distance, they also (at least in this model!) have more carbohydrates stored up in their body.
Figuring out your pace as a percent of your VO2 max sounds tricky, but it’s not too hard.
We can use your pace in an all-out two-mile race as a pretty good estimate of your VO2 max pace, then figure out your marathon pace as a percent. Haven’t run the two-mile since high school? Multiply your 5k time, in minutes, by 0.63 to get an estimate of what you can run for two miles.
Let’s say you’ve run 25:00 for the 5k and are hoping to run your next marathon under 4 hours in 3:55

1.  An 25:00 5k multiplied by 0.63 gives us about 15:57 for two miles, or 7:39 mile pace—that’s your VO2 max speed.
2.  Dividing your VO2 max pace by your goal marathon pace (9:00 per mile) tells us the relative intensity you’ll be running at. In this case, it’s 0.83, or 83% of your VO2 max. According to the scientific literature, most people are able to run a marathon at 60-85% of their VO2 max.

Elites and experienced runners tend to be on the higher end of this scale, and less experienced runners tend to be on the lower end.
Figuring out how much carbohydrates your muscles have stored up is more complicated—it’s not something we can estimate with easy-to-measure numbers like your 5k PR. Instead, we’ll create three different scenarios:

-   A conservative estimate which assumes our leg muscles don’t have very much stored carbs,
-   A middle-of-the-road estimate which uses typical carb storage values from scientific studies of endurance athletes, and
-   A generous estimate, which uses carb storage values from “carbo-loaded” athletes who have consumed a lot of carbs before their event.

With this information, we can predict a range of points when you’ll hit the wall.
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

In the graph above, the red curve depicts the conservative estimate, the orange curve depicts the typical estimate, and the green curve depicts the generous or “carbo-loaded” estimate. The gray shaded region represents the typical range of speeds that marathoners can maintain for the duration of the race—60-85% of their VO2 max.
Once we know what percentage of VO2 max pace we wish to run at for our marathon, we can draw a vertical line to each curve to determine where we’ll hit the wall in each scenario.
In our example from earlier of the 25:00 5k runner shooting for a 3:55 marathon, we know that he’s going to be running at 83% of VO2 max. In this case, drawing a vertical line (marked on the graph in dark gray) up from 83% of VO2 max pace shows that, in the conservative scenario, our runner will hit the wall at a bit over 17 miles into the race.
If his muscle energy stores are more in line with that of a trained athlete, he could make it all the way to 23 miles before he hits the wall. And if he’s done some carbo-loading in the days before the race and has above-average carbohydrate storage in his leg muscles, he could make it all the way to the finish without hitting the wall.

## Conclusion And Limitations​

Rapoport concludes his study with a few words of caution: this model _is_ a simplistic one, and glosses over some important details.
To name just one, this model assumes that all runners have about the same amount of muscle mass in terms of percentage of their body weight—something that isn’t true in the real world!
**Rapoport calculates that the uncertainty associated with his model should be around 5-10%, meaning our predicted “wall” timings could be off by one or two miles.**
Considering this, it’s best to use the conservative estimate—the red line—as a starting point for planning out your next marathon race. Also keep in mind that even this conservative estimate might be off by a mile or two.
So, you’ve done the math, checked the chart, and found out that you’re probably going to hit the wall in your next marathon. But what can you _do_ about it—how do you figure out how much carbs to take and when to take them? That’s what we’ll look at next time!

#### Who We Are​

Your team of expert coaches and fellow runners dedicated to helping you train smarter, stay healthy and run faster.
We love running and want to spread our expertise and passion to inspire, motivate, and help you achieve your running goals.

GET STARTED NOW

### References​

1\. Rapoport, B. I., Metabolic Factors Limiting Performance in Marathon Runners. PLoS Computational Biology 2010, 6 (10), e1000960.
2\. Maughan, R. J.; Leiper, J. B., Aerobic capacity and fractional utilisation of aerobic capacity in elite and non-elite male and female marathon runners. European Journal of Applied Physiology and Occupational Physiology 1983, 52 (1), 80-87.
3\. Daniels, J., Daniels' Running Formula. 2 ed.; Human Kinetics: 2005.

### JanSz — 2023-11-07T17:15:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-during-a-marathon.28302/post-327981>

Assume healthy 150# 5'8" toll, male running marathon and achieving the best to date marathon time.
How much energy he spends running that marathon?
Post that in kWh and HPh.
\------------------------------------------
A marathon is approximately 42.195 kilometers (26.219 miles).
The energy expenditure for running depends on the weight of the person and the distance they run.
[For a **person weighing 150 pounds** (approximately 68 kilograms), the total energy required to run a marathon is approximately **2950 kcal**](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1000960)**.**
[This is based on the energetic cost of running, which is approximately 1 kcal per kilogram of body mass per kilometer of running](https://runnersconnect.net/marathon-hitting-the-wall/).
Now, let’s convert this energy expenditure to kilowatt-hours (kWh) and horsepower-hours (HPh).
1 kcal = 0.00116222 kWh So, 2950 kcal = 2950 \* 0.00116222 = **3.428 kWh**
1 kcal = 0.00155961 HPh So, 2950 kcal = 2950 \* 0.00155961 = **4.601 HPh**
So, a 150-pound male running a marathon expends approximately **3.428 kWh or 4.601 HPh of energy.**
Please note that these are approximate values and actual energy expenditure can vary based on numerous factors including the runner’s efficiency, the course profile, and environmental conditions.

fastest marathon time

[who completed a marathon in](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1298762/fastest-men-marathon-times/)

### JanSz — 2023-11-07T17:24:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-during-a-marathon.28302/post-327982>

### [Should You Become a Fat Adapted Runner - Runners Connect](https://runnersconnect.net/should-you-become-a-fat-adapted-runner/)

Training isn’t the only part of Ultra or Marathon Racing you need to concern yourself with. In fact, the nutritional component can be as, if not more important, if you don't get it right. That said, there's an emerging trend among ultra and marathon runners to switch their diets to primarily...

 runnersconnect.net

### Should You Become a Fat Adapted Runner​

#### Coach Jeff​

Training isn’t the only part of Ultra or Marathon Racing you need to concern yourself with.
In fact, the nutritional component can be as, if not more important, if you don’t get it right.
That said, there’s an emerging trend among ultra and marathon runners to switch their diets to primarily fats, rather than carbohydrates, to become more “fat adapted”.
So, in this article we will discuss what that means, if it’s effective, how to do it, and whether you should try to become a fat adapted runner yourself.

### What Does It Mean To Be Fat Adapted​

Let’s start by examining some basic nutrition principles.
In order to run, your body needs to burn energy. Luckily, our body has **two primary forms of energy – carbohydrates and fats.** Unfortunately, both also have pluses and minuses.
Carbohydrates are burned very efficiently by the body since you can quickly break them down into usable energy.
The downside is that you can only store a finite amount of carbohydrates (not even close to enough to get you through a race) and therefore you need to be constantly replenishing them.
Fat, on the other hand, isn’t broken down by the body very efficiently. As such, the more energy your body demands (i.e. the faster you want to run) the harder it becomes for your body to use far.
The plus-side, however, is that you have more than enough fat stored in your body to last the entire race!
**Therefore, when we talk about becoming a fat adapted runner,
this means training the body to burn fat more efficiently as a fuel source.**

### Levels Of Fat Adaptation​

Now, there are also different “levels” of fat adaptation you can strive for accomplish.
On one end of the spectrum,
you have those that are living in a constant state of ketosis. This is when you constantly restrict carbohydrate intake so that you’re producing ketones (molecules your body uses to burn energy)
This is the highest level and when your body will become the most efficient at burning fat when you run.
On the lower end of the spectrum,
you have runners that only practice fat adaptation during their runs by fasting for 12-18 hours before their workout and not fueling during.
This will result in better fat burning adaptation, but not as much as a runner in ketosis.
In the middle of the spectrum
would be runners who adhere to a low carbohydrate diet at all times and who practice **fasting during their runs.**
The important distinction here is between a “low carbohydrate diet”, which usually means around 150g of carbohydrate intake, and ketosis, which is less than 50g of carbohydrates per day.

### Will Being Fat Adapted Help You Burn More Fat?​

Unfortunately, right now there is not a lot of research on ketogenic diets and endurance performance.
The ultimate study would be able to compare two runners performing the same distance at the same effort and measure the percentage of fats vs carbohydrates burned and cross section this with performance metrics like heart rate and running economy.
But, here is what we do have available right now.
One of the best studies on whether fat adapted diets do increase fat utilization was conduced by Stephen Phinney and Jeff Volek in 2016 (Fat-Adapted Substrate use in Trained Elite Runners).
This study showed that keto-adapted athletes burned around **1.2g/min of fat**, which is a far greater percentage than **the .76g/min** we normally see marathoners burn.
Furthermore, a study in 2016 by Tim Noakes compared the fat-burning ability of a keto group vs a “normal” higher carb group in a 2-hour ride at 70% VO2max.
Researchers found the keto group did indeed burn more fat (1.2g/min) compared to the high carb group (0.5g/min).

### You Can Burn More Fat, But Does That Lead To Better Performance?​

Okay, so now we know that fat-adapted athletes do indeed burn a greater percentage of fat when training and racing.
**But does this come at a cost to performance? Are the athletes working harder or need to slow down to achieve these results?**
That’s what the research group at the Australian Institute of Sport, led by Dr. Louise Burke PhD, set out to test
The study involved creating **three different groups of race walkers:**
a high carb group,
a carb restricted group and a keto group
and having them compete in both a 10km and 25km time trial.
The results once again demonstrated that the keto had higher rates of fat oxidation compared to the higher groups.
But, here’s the important part.
The keto group performed worse than there first 10km test
while the high-carb and periodized-carb group improved their race time by 6.6 and 5.3% respectively.
And in the 25km event,
the keto group had higher rates of exertion and higher heart rates
than the carbohydrate groups.
**To put more clearly, in 10km and 25km events, athletes who adhered to a ketogenic diet performed worse** than those who used a low carb or normal carb diet.
The researchers posit this happened because running economy is impacted when in ketosis.
While the body burns fat more efficiently, it still has to work hard to do so.
Now, a critical thing to keep in mind here is that the longest event was 25km.
Moreover, the participants were racewalking, not running.
So, we don’t fully understand how this data will translate to running events between a marathon to 100 miles.

### The Pros And Cons And Should You Try To Become Fat Adapted?​

My experience working with thousands of runners is that you should choose a diet option that best suits your lifestyle and personality.
A fully ketogenic diet is difficult to maintain and comes with its own set of challenges.
For some runners, the discipline required to maintain the diet is a welcome challenge that keeps the motivated. Likewise, some runners may feel healthier overall when they are on a ketogenic diet.
So, if you enjoy the ketogenic diet and feel strong and healthy, there’s no harm.
But, if sticking to such a rigid diet is difficult or you don’t feel like you have enough energy, then you certainly don’t need a ketogenic diet to benefit your racing.
As the research indicates,
from a pure performance perspective,
a full ketogenic diet will not enhance your performance.
In fact, Dr. Burke and her team recommend a more balanced approach to fat adaption, stating …

> A periodized programme that includes some training sessions deliberately undertaken with low carbohydrate availability or a delay in replacing muscle glycogen after a session may promote greater cellular adaptations to training and enhance performance to a greater magnitude than undertaking all sessions with high carb availability.

Based on my own coaching experience and the data gathered by research, I agree with this approach.

### How And When To Perform Fat Adapted Training​

We cover this more in our [Race Day Fueling Course](https://runnersconnect.net/marathon-nutrition-blueprint/), but here is a simple set of rules to follow…
If you are first starting with fat-adapted training, you should run your early training segment long runs in a glycogen depleted state.
I don’t recommend trying it for the first time on your first 20 or 22-miler.
This is because (1) we want to ease your body into the process to see how it reacts and (2) if you do struggle, we don’t want it to be during an important long run or workout.
Thus, I recommend using a fat-adapted strategy as early in the training cycle as possible and then up until your last two or three race specific long runs.
During these last few race specific long runs, I recommend practicing your race fueling strategy instead.
This will ensure you have ample time to tweak anything you need for race day.

### What Does This Mean For Your Race Day Fueling Plan​

The important part of all this information is really how you out it in practice.
If you are a member of our [Training Plans](https://runnersconnect.net/pro-signup/), [Masters](https://runnersconnect.net/pro-signup/) or [Marathon Nutrition Blueprint](https://runnersconnect.net/marathon-nutrition-blueprint/), here is how you adjust the specific calculators to account for the level of fat-adaptation training you employ.
For the marathon, you don’t change your fueling numbers at all.
This is because at the intensity of the marathon, here’s only a slight percentage point difference in how much carbohydrate you’re going to need to replace.
For the ultra marathon, we provide you two sets of data. The carbohydrates needed for a more traditional diet and the carbohydrates needed for a fat-adapted athlete.

-   If you practice no fat adaption methods, then choose the higher of the carbohydrate numbers.
-   If you practice some fat adaption methods, use the lower carbohydrate number.
-   If you consistently practice fat adaption methods in training and have a lower carbohydrate diet, you can use the high end fat adapted output.
-   If you are always in a state of ketosis, you can use the lower end of the fat-adapted calculation.

I hope this article helped you understand some of the science behind fat adaption training and how to best implement it in your training.

# Thread: How is weight loss achieved
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-weight-loss-achieved.28304/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ateale — 2023-11-08T17:10:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-weight-loss-achieved.28304/post-328027>

Hi everyone!

I have a friend who seems interested in some of the ideas related to restoring one's circadian rhythm.

He asked me how it's possible for people to be losing so much weight by simply improving their light diet and getting exposure to cold (CT).

This is what I currently understand more or less but I would appreciate any further notes/ideas/corrections so that I can pass it on to my friend and hopefully convince him to join these forums:

**Sunlight**
\- Exposure to blue light outside of sunlight hours ruins/interrupts circadian rhythm
\- Blue light stimulates blood glucose and insulin
\- Blue light turns off POMC (pro-opiomelanocordon) and expression whereas UV light increases POMC (Sunlight stimulates POMC which leads to the creation of melanin)
\- Red light preconditions your skin for UV

**Cold Thermogenesis**:
\- body generates heat when exposed to temperatures significantly below its optimum temperature
\- it accelerates various metabolic processes, such as energy creation, fat burning, digestion, and muscle activity
\- Water condenses, causing the condensation to contract around mitochondria make it more energy efficient and increases electrical and magnetic fields, causing us to burn more calories

**Eat like a great white shark**
\- proteins / fats contain more more (and less deuterium than compared to carbs/sugars) allow for our mitochondria to produce more water that is lower in deuterium ?

**POMC**
\- gene or group of hormones???
\- regulates melanin produced from melanocytes in the skin
\- POMC levels are regulated by sunlight (effected by time of year / season / location)

### ateale — 2023-11-08T17:17:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-weight-loss-achieved.28304/post-328028>

Cold exposure stimulates leptin too?

**Leptin**
\- underneath our subcutaneous fat
\- is responsible for regulating the energy balance in the body - communicates with the hypothalamus -> suprachiasmatic nucleus (central clock of the body?) + pineal gland?

**Melatonin**
\- produced by pineal gland when melanopsin isn't signalling to it (this happens at night / when there is not sunlight)
\- tells your body that it is nighttime, cell repair / cleanup begins

### JanSz — 2023-11-08T17:55:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-weight-loss-achieved.28304/post-328033>

I have a friend who seems interested in some of the ideas related to **restoring one's circadian rhythm**.
\=================================================================
One thing about good circadian rhythm is good cortisol levels over the night and day.
One of promoted ways of achieving that is having early morning outdoor exposure.
(On purpose I am not mentioning that we must look at the actual sun.)
\-------
Testing often is discouraged, but I like to test.
I like DUTCHtest.com
\----------------------------------
When you do that test,
you may find out that your cortisol pattern is weak.

**But keep looking at your test results.**
You may find out that your adrenals are **making heaps of cortisol**
but
most of it is immediately converted to cortisone (the inactive part).

Chewing on licorice may adjust that conversion toward your benefit.

# Thread: Diabetic retinopathy stoichiometry defines most traumatic brain injuries.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diabetic-retinopathy-stoichiometry-defines-most-traumatic-brain-injuries.28313/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-11-09T20:55:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diabetic-retinopathy-stoichiometry-defines-most-traumatic-brain-injuries.28313/post-328107>

Person with diabetes has a high fasting insulin.
Question:
**What fasting insulin level healthy person should have?**
I am hearing that **it should be 2-3** but definitely less than 5.5.
What is your opinion? Or what are you aiming for yourself?

LabCorp
2.6−24.9 μIU/mL

### [004333: Insulin | Labcorp](https://www.labcorp.com/tests/004333/insulin)

Labcorp test details for Insulin

 www.labcorp.com

\---------------------------------------------------------------------
The quote:
Diabetic retinopathy stoichiometry defines most traumatic brain injuries.
is from your post here:

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tbi-thread.26146/#post-328090](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tbi-thread.26146/#post-328090)

\----------------------------------------------------------------------------
**Diabetic retinopathy** is a complication of diabetes that affects the eyes. It is caused by damage to the blood vessels of the retina, which is the light-sensitive tissue at the back of the eye. [This damage can lead to vision loss or blindness if left untreated](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fopht.2022.1012804/full).

**Traumatic brain injury (TBI)** is a type of brain injury that occurs when an external force, such as a blow to the head, causes damage to the brain. [Symptoms of TBI can range from mild to severe and can include headaches, dizziness, confusion, memory loss, and more](https://www.aao.org/eyenet/article/traumatic-brain-injury-visual-disorders-what-every-2).

> **high blood pressure**

One of the major reasons for high blood pressure is being older than 40yo.

[According to different sources, **the percentage of adults over 40 who have high blood pressure ranges from 33.2% to 74.5%**](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=16b3272e275461c4JmltdHM9MTcwMTEyOTYwMCZpZ3VpZD0zMTBiZjgxOS0wYTdmLTZhMTEtMGU1Mi1lYmNhMGJkNzZiNjgmaW5zaWQ9NTc3MA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=3&fclid=310bf819-0a7f-6a11-0e52-ebca0bd76b68&psq=half+of+the+people+with+high+blood+pressure+are+usually+40+years+old+and+older&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYWFycC5vcmcvaGVhbHRoL2NvbmRpdGlvbnMtdHJlYXRtZW50cy9pbmZvLTIwMTcvY2RjLWJsb29kLXByZXNzdXJlLWZkLmh0bWw&ntb=1)[1](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=8ef58d613d6abc9cJmltdHM9MTcwMTEyOTYwMCZpZ3VpZD0zMTBiZjgxOS0wYTdmLTZhMTEtMGU1Mi1lYmNhMGJkNzZiNjgmaW5zaWQ9NTc3MQ&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=3&fclid=310bf819-0a7f-6a11-0e52-ebca0bd76b68&psq=half+of+the+people+with+high+blood+pressure+are+usually+40+years+old+and+older&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYWFycC5vcmcvaGVhbHRoL2NvbmRpdGlvbnMtdHJlYXRtZW50cy9pbmZvLTIwMTcvY2RjLWJsb29kLXByZXNzdXJlLWZkLmh0bWw&ntb=1)[2](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=64333728bfcff447JmltdHM9MTcwMTEyOTYwMCZpZ3VpZD0zMTBiZjgxOS0wYTdmLTZhMTEtMGU1Mi1lYmNhMGJkNzZiNjgmaW5zaWQ9NTc3Mg&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=3&fclid=310bf819-0a7f-6a11-0e52-ebca0bd76b68&psq=half+of+the+people+with+high+blood+pressure+are+usually+40+years+old+and+older&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY2RjLmdvdi9uY2hzL3Byb2R1Y3RzL2RhdGFicmllZnMvZGIzNjQuaHRt&ntb=1).

\-----------
The immediate reason for high blood pressure are:
lack of nitric oxide in the blood or just lower NO levels, lover than optimal.
Resulting in rigid (not-expanding, not-flexible) cardiovascular system.
Think how the car would behave if you removed shock absorbers. Or welded them solid.

The other are deposits on the interior surfaces of cardiovascular system, (reducing available volume.

I suspect that ADH (Antidiuretic Hormone), vasopressin
levels play's role too (by adjusting total blood volume level).

Also, body volume must be playing significant role.

Body bones amount and cardiovascular system volume
I think are rather steady.
But in 1900 average man was weighting 140 -150#
in 2023 that is usually above 200#

Statins increase cardiovascular calcifications.
(and reduce cardiovascular volume, increasing need for higher blood pressure).
\-------------
Thank you to abundance of (bad) food.
Thank you to seed oils.

# Thread: A little overview over different training modalities
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-little-overview-over-different-training-modalities.28318/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sfast — 2023-11-11T14:31:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-little-overview-over-different-training-modalities.28318/post-328152>

Hi Folks,

this image (copyright by me) is part of the generalised model of fitness I am working on:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Some commentary:​

\- This is why most bodybuilders are sick and why their intuition to do fasted cardio first thing in the morning is right: You need to earn Zone 4 by being fit. Otherwise you can't support higher intensity stuff.
\- The energy usage is a statement of an ideal situation. If you are sick, you will burn carbs even in zone 1. This provides a rough guideline how to progress the training. You start by increasing Walking, NEAT (Crafting is AWESOME exercise), get your leptin sensitivity etc. Then you introduce Jogging, Dancing, Light Exercise like Yoga etc. Wait until you can burn fat there, too. Then Strength + Athletic Training (it can be scaled to almost any situations) but keep the training mode towards low reps (intensity as needed), high sets. And then you might earn your way towards Zone 4.
\- CT w/o shivering is Zone 1/2, CT + Shivering is Zone 3. So, this might contradict Dr K. Either my model needs to be worked, I contradict Dr K or I am wrong and shivering is still Zone 2.
\- Notice that strength is CP + Fat. No high reps with fatigue to be found. In my experience, this increases exercise tolerance for people who want to train a lot or cannot train a lot because of sickness.
\- Zone 1/2 and big part of Zone 3 is outdoor exercise by nature.

### rk257 — 2024-12-10T19:24:38+0200

# Thread: LF meter
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lf-meter.28338/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### coho3131 — 2023-11-17T02:04:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lf-meter.28338/post-328357>

The first Gigahertz Solutions LF meter I am looking at goes from 16 Hz to 2,000 Hz.

The second Gigahertz Solutions meter I am looking at is a little more professional, but only goes from 16 Hz to 1,000 Hz.

If I get the second meter, what sources of waves will I be missing in the 1,000 Hz to 2,000 Hz range?

Thank you.

### JanSz — 2023-11-17T02:49:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lf-meter.28338/post-328358>

> [coho3131 said:](/goto/post?id=328357)
>
> The first Gigahertz Solutions LF meter I am looking at goes from 16 Hz to 2,000 Hz.
>
> There second Gigahertz Solutions meter I am looking at is a little more professional, but only goes from 16 Hz to 1,000 Hz.
>
> If I get the second meter, **what sources of waves will I be missing in the 1,000 Hz to 2,000 Hz range?**
>
> Thank you.

**sources of waves 1000 Hz to 2000 Hz**

[The frequency range of 1,000 Hz to 2,000 Hz falls within the **audible range** of human hearing](https://phys.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/University_Physics/Book%3A_University_Physics_%28OpenStax%29/Book%3A_University_Physics_I_-_Mechanics_Sound_Oscillations_and_Waves_%28OpenStax%29/17%3A_Sound/17.04%3A_Sound_Intensity).
[This range is also known as the **midrange** and is responsible for the perception of sounds such as musical instruments, human speech, and birdsongs](https://blog.sonicbids.com/understanding-frequencies-how-to-describe-what-youre-hearing-to-your-sound-tech).
Sources of waves in this frequency range include:

-   **Musical instruments**: The midrange is where most musical instruments produce their fundamental frequencies.
-   [For example, the piano’s middle C note has a frequency of 261.63 Hz, which is within this range](https://phys.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/University_Physics/Book%3A_University_Physics_%28OpenStax%29/Book%3A_University_Physics_I_-_Mechanics_Sound_Oscillations_and_Waves_%28OpenStax%29/17%3A_Sound/17.04%3A_Sound_Intensity).
-   **Human speech**: The fundamental frequencies of most human speech sounds fall within the midrange.
-   [This range is crucial for the perception of consonants and vowels](https://phys.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/University_Physics/Book%3A_University_Physics_%28OpenStax%29/Book%3A_University_Physics_I_-_Mechanics_Sound_Oscillations_and_Waves_%28OpenStax%29/17%3A_Sound/17.04%3A_Sound_Intensity).
-   **Birdsongs**: Many bird species produce songs that fall within the midrange.
-   [For example, the American Robin’s song has a frequency range of 2,500 Hz to 3,500 Hz, which overlaps with this range](https://blog.sonicbids.com/understanding-frequencies-how-to-describe-what-youre-hearing-to-your-sound-tech).

# Thread: PT in El Savador? You better believe it!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pt-in-el-savador-you-better-believe-it.28343/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dr. Mimi — 2023-11-19T19:42:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pt-in-el-savador-you-better-believe-it.28343/post-328443>

Physical therapy in El Salvador? The beaches south of Libertad are flat. We’re staying in Playa San Diego seven steps from the beach. My son walked only 10 ft. with a walker in the blue lit PT facility on Tuesday in Philadelphia. After an exhausting day of travel, he rolled around in the ocean for a couple of hours in the afternoon and then walked about ¾ of a football field with the walker on Thursday. And took 20 steps in the ocean without the walker on Friday. The healing is palpable! On our way to Los Pelicanos for lunch…

### NikkiYork — 2023-11-30T05:22:45+0200
