# Thread: Medical Examination Requirements for Former Astronauts
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/medical-examination-requirements-for-former-astronauts.28348/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-11-20T22:35:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/medical-examination-requirements-for-former-astronauts.28348/post-328492>

### [Medical Examination Requirements (MER) for Former Astronauts - NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/)

Tests: Complete Blood Count – hemoglobin, hematocrit, red blood cell count, red blood cell indices, white blood cell count, differential count, platelet

 www.nasa.gov

### Medical Examination Requirements (MER) for Former Astronauts​

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

#### Robert E. Lewis​

#### Web Community Manager​

MAR 16, 2023
ARTICLE

### CONTENTS​

-   [Laboratory Testing](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-1)
-   [Musculoskeletal](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-2)
-   [Dermatology](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-3)
-   [Ophthalmology/Optometry](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-4)
-   [Audiology](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-5)
-   [Cardiopulmonary](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-6)
-   [Gastroenterology](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-7)
-   [Reproductive Health](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-8)
-   [Behavioral Health](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/hhp/medical-examination-requirements-mer-for-former-astronauts/#section-9)

Bioanalytical Core Lab
NASA

### Laboratory Testing​

#### MER1001 Hematology (annually)​

**Tests:** Complete Blood Count – hemoglobin, hematocrit, red blood cell count, red blood cell indices, white blood cell count, differential count, platelet count.
**Rationale:** The results of this test are used to diagnose conditions and diseases such as anemia, infection, and thrombocytopenia. There currently is no occupational surveillance activity, associated with an exposure-identified occupational health risk, that requires the information from this test. This screening is a standard of care and is included in the annual Lifetime Surveillance of Astronaut Health (LSAH) examination. These data are also needed to support Epidemiology’s astronaut surveillance activities for detection of unknown adverse health conditions that may be related to occupational exposures.

#### MER1002 Biochemistry (annually)​

**Tests:**

-   Liver function – AST, ALT, GGT, bilirubin, ALP, LDH
-   Renal function – urea, creatinine, electrolytes (Na, K, Cl), uric acid
-   Endocrine – TSH, free T4
-   Fasting blood glucose and hemoglobin A1c
-   Cardiovascular profile – fasting total cholesterol, HDL, LDL, non-HDL
-   cholesterol, triglycerides
-   HS-CRP
-   Calcium, magnesium, inorganic phosphate
-   Prostate-specific antigen (males) beginning at age 50
-   Iron, total iron-binding capacity, percentage saturation of transferrin, ferritin

**Rationale:** A chemistry panel determines an astronaut’s general health status, elucidating the body’s electrolyte balance, arbohydrate, lipid status, and status of several major body organs, such as the kidney, liver, and thyroid gland. The long-term effects of radiation and microgravity exposure are poorly understood. Although the short-term health effects of radiation exposure seem benign in low Earth orbit, the long-term effects of exposure to relatively low doses of radiation on various organ systems are unknown and need to be monitored. The United States Preventative Services Task Force (USPSTF) Guideline: The USPSTF strongly recommends screening men aged 35 and older for lipid disorders. The USPSTF strongly recommends screening women aged 45 and older for lipid disorders if they are at increased risk for coronary heart disease.

View of Dan Burbank,Expedition 30 Commander; and European Space Agency (ESA) Andre Kuipers,Expedition 30 Flight Engineer (FE),during Integrated Immune Blood Sample Draw at the Human Research Facility (HRF),in the Columbus Module. Photo was taken during Expedition 30.
NASA

#### MER1003 Urinalysis (annually)​

**Test:** Standard urinalysis test strip.
**Rationale:** There currently is no occupational health risk surveillance activity associated with an identified occupational exposure, that requires the information from this test. This screening is a standard of care and is included in the annual LSAH examination. These data are also needed to support Epidemiology’s astronaut surveillance activities for detection of unknown adverse health conditions that may be related to occupational exposures.

View of personnel at work with InVia Raman Microscope in Bldg 13 Chemical Analysis lab rm 258.
NASA

### Musculoskeletal​

#### MER2001 Bone Health (Every 3 years)​

**Test:** Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) and bone health serologic markers.
**Rationale:** CHS manages an astronaut-specific bone health program through the JSC clinic. This aspect of long-term astronaut monitoring is unique to NASA and the astronaut corps. Concerns exist that exposure to the space flight environment (microgravity, space radiation, and elevated CO2 levels) may cause a decrease in bone strength and, thus, an increase in the risk of non-traumatic fractures for astronauts relative to healthy non-astronauts. A major risk to crew health is the change in bone ultimate strength with respect to mechanical loads; it is possible that a fracture may occur from activities that would be unlikely to induce fracture before space flight. From an occupational health perspective, occupational surveillance needs to be conducted throughout the lifetime of astronauts to detect health issues and develop mitigation strategies to reduce the risk.

### Dermatology​

#### MER3001 Clinical Evaluation (Annually)​

**Test:** Visual exam of the skin with photo documentation of any abnormalities.
**Rationale:** Although the difference is not statistically significant, the rate of melanoma skin cancers are 59% higher in astronauts than in a control group. The incidence rate of diagnosed non-melanoma skin cancers appears to be increased post-flight. The incidence of non-melanomas skin cancers is not reported to cancer registries; therefore, the literature regarding its incidence in the general population is limited. Because of the limited documentation, this exam is needed to gather data to better understand the incidence rate in the astronaut population. Participants are referred to a dermatologist when results of the exam indicate the presence of potential skin cancer.

View of Urine Collection Device (UCD) – Male Bellows Catheter,Serial Number (S/N): A5577,Barcode: HRF07140J,and Kona Coffee drink container in the Columbus Module. Photo was taken during Expedition 30.
NASA

### Ophthalmology/Optometry​

#### MER4001 Assessment of Visual Function (Annually)​

**Test:** Visual acuity, color vision, and extraocular muscles.
**Rationale:** An ophthalmologic examination measures the astronaut’s ocular health and visual status, in order to detect abnormalities in the components of the visual system and to determine how well the person can see. It may also reveal the presence of hypertension, increased intracranial pressure, or diabetes. The lens of the eye is recognized as one of the most radiosensitive tissues in the human body, and it is known that cataracts can be induced by acute doses of less than 2 Gy of lowLET ionizing radiation and less than 5 Gy of protracted radiation. Although much work has been carried out in this area, the exact mechanisms of radiation cataractogenesis are still not fully understood. In particular, the question of the threshold dose for cataract development is not resolved. Cataracts have been classified as a deterministic effect of radiation exposure with a threshold of approximately 2 Gy. Studies indicate the threshold for cataract development is less than was previously estimated, on the order of 0.5 Gy, and that radiation cataractogenesis may, in fact, be more accurately described by a linear, no-threshold model. There appears to be no relationship between radiation exposure and the progression rate of an aggregate area of posterior subcapsular cataract or nuclear cataract. However, longer follow-up is needed to further understand any impact of space radiation on progression rates for these types of cataracts and to characterize changes in visual acuity. Cataract formation rates are being characterized by the Epidemiology group.

#### MER4002 Refraction Manifest/Cycloplegic (Annually)​

**Test:** Manifest/Cycloplegic refraction test.
**Rationale:** Refraction tests are used to measure refractive errors (myopia–nearsightedness, hyperopia – farsightedness, and astigmatism), and determine a person’s prescription for eyeglasses or contact lenses. As reported in the postflight exam vision questionnaire, 50 percent of astronauts living on the International Space Station have reported vision changes related to flight. However, long-term effects of vision changes secondary to microgravity are not well understood. From an occupational health perspective, occupational surveillance needs to be conducted throughout the lifetime of astronauts to detect ocular health issues and develop mitigation strategies to reduce the risk.

Buzz Aldrin – ST105.012
NASA

#### MER4003 Intraocular Pressure (IOP) (Annually)​

**Test:** Tonometry test
**Rationale:** Elevated IOP has been observed during parabolic flight and during microgravity exposure. The clinical significance of this rise in IOP in microgravity is unknown. However, it is theorized that a prolonged increase in IOP during space flight could put astronauts at risk for optic nerve damage. Those at risk for glaucomatous optic nerve damage would most likely be those who were exposed to microgravity for months to years. From an occupational health perspective, occupational surveillance needs to be conducted throughout the lifetime of astronauts to detect ocular health issues and develop mitigation strategies to reduce the risk.

#### MER4004 Ocular Health Assessment – Imaging (Annually)​

**Test:** Dilated fundus exam, retinal photographs, and optical coherence tomography (OCT)
**Rationale:** There are many concerns related to the ocular system, cephalad fluid shift during microgravity exposure and potential increased intracranial pressure being chief among these concerns. Permanent hyperopic shifts have been documented after long-duration flights. Additionally, optic disc edema, globe flattening, and choroidal folds have been documented in these astronauts. Some individuals experienced transient changes post flight while others have reported persistent changes with varying degrees of severity. The exact cause(s) and long-term effects of these phenomena are not known at this time. From an occupational health perspective, occupational surveillance needs to be conducted throughout the lifetime of astronauts to detect health issues and develop mitigation strategies to reduce the risk.

NASA astronaut Karen Nyberg, Expedition 36 flight engineer, conducts an ocular health exam on herself in the Destiny laboratory of the Earth-orbiting International Space Station. (NASA)
NASA

### Audiology​

#### MER5001 Clinical Evaluation/Audiometry (Annually)​

**Test:** Hearing questionnaire and pure-tone audiometry test
**Rationale:** NASA is concerned about strategies to assess and reduce acute, chronic, and delayed effects of hearing loss due to terrestrial (ground-based) and space flight noise exposures experienced by astronauts. There is a significant concern about synergistic interactions between noise, vibration, workload, and comorbidity factors of radiation, toxicology, and microgravity on auditory status. The long-term effects of these occupational exposures are unknown. A hearing questionnaire and pure-tone audiometry test can also help identify progressive changes in auditory function that warrant additional diagnostic evaluations or treatment. As a result, annual audiometric tests will continue to be included as part of the annual exams.

NASA astronaut Barry Wilmore conducts a Tonometry exam on NASA astronaut Terry Virts. Tonometry measures the intra-ocular pressure of the subject.
NASA

### Cardiopulmonary​

#### MER6001 Hypertension Screening (annually and as clinically indicated)​

**Test:** Hypertension screening using a sphygmomanometer.
**Rationale:** This screening is based on the United States Preventative Services Task Force (USPSTF) guidelines. There currently is no occupational health risk associated with hypertension. This screening is a standard of care and is included in the annual Lifetime Surveillance of Astronaut Health examination. These data are also needed to support Epidemiology’s astronaut surveillance activities for detection of unknown adverse health conditions that may be related to occupational exposures. USPSTF Guideline: The USPSTF recommends screening for high blood pressure in adults aged 18 and older.

#### MER6002 Resting 12-lead ECG (Annually)​

**Test:** Resting 12-lead electrocardiogram.
**Rationale:** There are concerns that astronauts may have subclinical cardiac abnormalities that could be exacerbated by the adaptive responses of the cardiovascular system to microgravity. Currently, the long-term effect of these adaptive responses is not known. This test is required to obtain the data needed to determine whether exacerbation of subclinical cardiac abnormalities is a valid long-term impact of occupational exposure to space flight. Knowing this may have implications for long-duration space missions in the future.

Mission Specialist Acaba used the shuttle’s ergometer.
NASA

#### MER6003 Cardiovascular Health Screening (Annually)​

**Test:** Cardiovascular health screening.
**Rationale:** The cardiovascular health screening documents cardiovascular risk factors (blood pressure, age, gender, cholesterol, smoking status) and includes CIMT calculation. These risk factors are used to calculate a Framingham Risk Score. The Framingham Risk Score is a gender-specific algorithm used to estimate the 10-year cardiovascular risk of an individual. Astronauts may have subclinical cardiac abnormalities that could be exacerbated by the adaptive responses of the cardiovascular system to microgravity and exposure to space radiation. We do not currently know the long-term effect of these adaptive responses. This test is required to obtain the data needed to determine whether exacerbation of subclinical cardiac abnormalities is a valid long-term impact of occupational exposure to space flight. Knowing this may have implications for long-duration space missions in the future.

### Gastroenterology​

This medical exam is done as clinically indicated.

### Reproductive Health​

This medical exam is done as clinically indicated.

### Behavioral Health​

This medical exam is done as clinically indicated.

////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

..

### JanSz — 2023-11-20T22:50:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/medical-examination-requirements-for-former-astronauts.28348/post-328493>

[**The Framingham Risk Score** is a sex-specific algorithm used to estimate the 10-year cardiovascular risk of an individual](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Framingham_Risk_Score)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Framingham_Risk_Score). [It was first developed based on data obtained from the Framingham Heart Study](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Framingham_Risk_Score)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Framingham_Risk_Score)[, which began in 1948](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Framingham_Risk_Score)[2](https://ukcab.net/resources/factsheets/framingham/). [The score is considered a reliable, accurate benchmark for assessing cardiovascular risk in the general population](https://ukcab.net/resources/factsheets/framingham/)[2](https://ukcab.net/resources/factsheets/framingham/).

[The ‘Hard’ coronary Framingham outcomes model is intended for use in non-diabetic patients aged 30-79 years with no prior history of coronary heart disease or intermittent claudication](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/38/framingham-risk-score-hard-coronary-heart-disease)[3](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/38/framingham-risk-score-hard-coronary-heart-disease). [It estimates the 10-year risk of heart attack based on factors such as age, sex, cholesterol levels, HDL levels, blood pressure, and smoking status](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/38/framingham-risk-score-hard-coronary-heart-disease)[3](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/38/framingham-risk-score-hard-coronary-heart-disease). This calculator was created by Dr. Peter W.F. [Wilson, a leading expert in coronary heart disease epidemiology and genetics](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/38/framingham-risk-score-hard-coronary-heart-disease)[3](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/38/framingham-risk-score-hard-coronary-heart-disease).

# Thread: Does red light or IR light help with Osteoarthritis?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-red-light-or-ir-light-help-with-osteoarthritis.28349/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### annelies_1 — 2023-11-21T16:58:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-red-light-or-ir-light-help-with-osteoarthritis.28349/post-328504>

I read a lot about RLT or IR and osteoarthritis. I know that the disease ultimately stems from low redox, but can RLT or IR help in any way? And if yes, should the person then do red light or infrared light?

Thanks

### tallweeds — 2023-11-21T17:59:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-red-light-or-ir-light-help-with-osteoarthritis.28349/post-328505>

Yes, and CT or whole body cyro therapy works great too.

### annelies_1 — 2023-11-22T00:03:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-red-light-or-ir-light-help-with-osteoarthritis.28349/post-328522>

Thanks! Would you say red light or IR light is better? Looking to buy a Christmas present for someone

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-22T01:35:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-red-light-or-ir-light-help-with-osteoarthritis.28349/post-328527>

> [annelies\_1 said:](/goto/post?id=328522)
>
> Thanks! Would you say red light or IR light is better? Looking to buy a Christmas present for someone

Red light (that you can see) is for skin, it only penetrates a few mm. NIR 850nm +/- goes in 2-3 inches.

### Michalis — 2023-11-23T05:17:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-red-light-or-ir-light-help-with-osteoarthritis.28349/post-328560>

Sun helps my joints a bit however no artificial light has done much of a difference. It won't fix or reverse osteoarthritis though. You can tell the person in question to add other modalities like CT and the effect multiplies but again it has not fixed it for me and I don't think it will at least in my current environment. The only thing I haven't tried is moving long term somewhere with low population density, low blue lit and low emf. Maybe this is the variable you need to be cognisant about and let the person know. Then it all boils down to the person's obligations and if they can easily leave everything behind and move in order to give that a go. Which is my opinion the biggest hurdle especially if there are family matters involved.
Again on paper and in theory everything looks good from what we read here. In practice it is a completely different story as everyone is differently built and sometimes the scales have also tipped so I believe no reversal can be achieved. So timing is very important.

### JanSz — 2023-11-23T16:13:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-red-light-or-ir-light-help-with-osteoarthritis.28349/post-328575>

> [annelies\_1 said:](/goto/post?id=328504)
>
> I read a lot about RLT or IR and osteoarthritis. I know that the disease ultimately stems from low redox, but can RLT or IR help in any way? And if yes, should the person then do red light or infrared light?
>
> Thanks

Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease that happens when the tissues that cushion the ends of the bones within the joints break down over time. It is the most common form of arthritis that affects joints in the hand, spine, knees and hips.
Symptoms of Osteoarthritis include pain and stiffness of joints (hand, knees, hip), tender and warm joints, swelling, limited range of movement.
\---------------------------------------------------------
Use joints less, especially the leg joints and lumbar vertebra, by weighting less.
Keep in mind that walking or running creates dynamic load (not static).
That dynamic load is up to six time higher than static.
Somebody who is 150# and runs, 150# time six=900 pounds
Same person, same bones, same joints but when the weight is 250#
250\*6=1500 pounds
If you have a car that is able to pull 3000# trailer and you hook up 6000# trailer, it will not go very far.

More than two-thirds of U.S. adults in the United States are overweight or have obesity.

Why Jack broke his tendon by just walking, and talks about it every time he speaks?
He was 350# (or more).
\--------------------------
Happy Thanksgiving


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Us 2 hick Canadians went down for a vacation and secondarily an ark research mission. We had a blast.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Naturally beautiful. The rural air and water feels clean and pristine. We drove ~500km stayed in 6 different locations different elevations. We learned our first dozen or so spanish words on the flight down . The language barrier was heavy of course, but everyone was so helpful, so happy, and often even amused by our communication incompetency. The food is way better than we even hoped for. I wouldn't say the food is cheap, but good value for the quality.

Our hotels/Airbnb's were as advertised. No hot water which surprised us but not a big deal.

Tbc..

### tomstaplez — 2023-11-21T21:22:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/our-ark-el-salvador-1-week-findings.28353/post-328513>

We aren't huge beach or night life people, but did experience both.

Kerri was likely mad at me thinking I was constantly looking for bikini's but I was actually looking for Jack.

### tomstaplez — 2023-11-21T21:36:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/our-ark-el-salvador-1-week-findings.28353/post-328514>

We think the only way we could board this ark is if we could acquire some accessible south facing land, 2500-4000' up, <1hr from ocean, <2hr from airport.

I'd build a few smaller airbnb style short term rentals. And cater to Canadians.

I'm currently going thru a midlife crisis, detest our government because they detest me, we currently host an airbnb here on the farm, we love to host know what it takes, I'm a confident builder, built a many houses from the ground up, we MIGHT have adequate capital to make it all happen.

I was talking to an Australian entrepreneur on the beach, he has been there for a year or two I think. But he didn't have the kindest words for Latin American culture and so on. Mildy told me I should look elsewhere.

Anyone care to talk about this opportunity we are considering? Thoughts? Suggestions? Experience? Legality of it all? Anyone try to build anything down there?
Gracious. Tom.

# Thread: antibiotic carbadox, pigs & humans
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotic-carbadox-pigs-humans.28354/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-11-22T14:42:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotic-carbadox-pigs-humans.28354/post-328536>

-   November 7, 2023, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration announced it is considering withdrawing approval for the **antibiotic carbadox**, which is added to pig feed to prevent infections and fatten up the animals, **due to cancer concerns**
-   The drug has been banned in the European Union since 1999, and in Canada since 2006. China, Brazil, Australia and the UK have also banned it due to concerns over its cancer risks
-   In 2014, the United Nation’s Codex Committee on Residues of Veterinary Drugs in Foods determined that **there is no safe level** of residues of carbadox or its metabolites in food that represents an acceptable **risk to consumers.**

### [FDA moves to pull common drug used by pork industry, citing human cancer risk](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/fda-carbadox-drug-pork-industry-human-cancer-risk/)

The manufacturer of the drug called carbadox claims the move is not "based on solid science."

 www.cbsnews.com

### JanSz — 2023-11-22T15:02:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotic-carbadox-pigs-humans.28354/post-328538>

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-i-had-extra-oysters-tonight-and-made.12531/post-151767](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-i-had-extra-oysters-tonight-and-made.12531/post-151767)

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=151767)
>
> Melt butter ( one stick) in a large saucepan over medium heat. Saute onion and celery (one pint diced) until tender I add one two two diced carrots too, about 10 minutes. I add lots of garlic but it is your choice how much you like. It is diced. I'd say I put in 1/4 cup in this one. Pour in the seafood or chicken stock (cup to pint), and reduce heat to low. Cook for about 15 minutes. Add the artichokes (2 cups diced) and oysters (two dozen), and simmer for 10 more minutes. Take half out and add one cup of heavy cream and put in vitamix and blend. I add Lemon juice of one lemon and its zest, tumeric, black pepper, garlic, oregano, rosemary, sage, more diced onion and celery with some reserved artichoke chunks with some shrooms. Finally, stir in the heavy cream (another cup if you need to make a few more servings) Salt to taste. To thicken you can use egg yolk or parmeasean. I used the cheese tonight cause I had some left over from NYE party. Add back the Vitamix stuff to the blend you just made so the soup has some chunks in it. Cook until heated through, but do not boil, about 15 minutes o cook down. Serve immediately. If you want to make it a sauce add more cheese or yolks to thicken and pout over anything cause it rocks.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=151771)
>
> Here is the side dish I made:
>
> Pork loin wrapped in bacon stuffed with cream cheese and jalapeño

# Thread: vacation, Akumal vs PDC

**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3

### coho3131 — 2023-11-26T02:20:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation-akumal-vs-pdc.28365/post-328660>

Any issues with Akumal?

### Michalis — 2023-11-26T08:11:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation-akumal-vs-pdc.28365/post-328663>

Went to MX twice. You need to rent something right at the beach if you want to have peace of mind and no issues accessing it without having your balls busted. I wrote my impressions on my journal a few years ago so check it out.
Personally I loved the beaches but Mexico is a ball ache. i would not stay in PDC. Go south and find something with easy beach access or just pay the money for the convenience if you can afford it.
By the way, Bacalar is lovely if you fancy a lake and it is really quiet.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### JanSz — 2023-11-26T18:08:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/run-for-your-life-at-a-comfortable-pace-and-not-too-far.28367/post-328678>

Those with between 71 to 80 beats per minute had a 51 percent greater chance of kicking the bucket during that period than those with a resting rate of under 50 beats.
At 81 to 90 beats per minute, that risk was double.
Over 90, and it tripled.

### JanSz — 2023-11-26T20:50:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/run-for-your-life-at-a-comfortable-pace-and-not-too-far.28367/post-328687>

incidents of heart diseases prior to 1900

According to 2, heart disease was uncommon in the early years of the 20th century.
In 1900, it was the fourth most common cause of death, trailing infectious diseases such as pneumonia and tuberculosis.
Three decades later, heart disease deaths had increased to become the commonest cause of death in the US.
Heart disease deaths continued to increase until the mid-1960s 3.
Experts believe that the main reason for the change in the frequency of heart disease is diet.
In pre-industrial societies, the typical person would eat more natural foods such as unprocessed dairy and whole grains.
**When machines arrived,** they encouraged us to make richer foods that are harder on the heart.
Processed dairy products, burgers, and fries in particular have grown more popular.
Part of this popularity is due to the short amount of time required for preparation.
**As fast food became more convenient and widely accepted, the rate of heart disease increased** 2.

### JanSz — 2023-11-26T20:57:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/run-for-your-life-at-a-comfortable-pace-and-not-too-far.28367/post-328688>

**In 1900 the norm was 140# man.

......**
In 1900, the average weight of a man was reported to be around **140 pound**s1.
However, it’s important to note that these averages can vary based on factors such as region, diet, and lifestyle.
For instance, a report from 1898 stated that the **average weight of a man was 140 poun**ds and the average **height was about 5 feet 6 inches**1.
By the early 20th century, the average weight for men had increased.
For example, in 1912, **men who were about 5 feet 9-10 inches tall averaged around 170 pounds**2.

5'10" 170# BMI=24.39
''''''''''''''''''''''
Note;
today we same genetics as 100 years ago.
It is not genetics that cause obesities.

///

### JanSz — 2023-11-26T21:13:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/run-for-your-life-at-a-comfortable-pace-and-not-too-far.28367/post-328690>

# Thread: Sensitive vs no sensitivity

**Posts:** 9

### jeremiasbtome — 2023-11-26T23:51:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sensitive-vs-no-sensitivity.28370/post-328693>

Before February I would use tech daily as the normal person does without turning on airplane mode or any safety precautions. I felt normal and had no aware health issues other than myopia and tourettes which were not bad.
Then after february I began living more like a black swan mitochondriac, being outside as much as possible, using fire at night, emf precautions (airplane mode, etc). Now months later I have gotten more sun than any other year possible, am 19 years old. Ate more fish and meat than any other year. And changed environent to lower latitude and daily hot sun compared to being in a high latitude.
Basically did as much right as possible. But ever since doing the lifestyle I became sensitive to tech, using laptop or phone, I feel the radiation and heart pain sometimes and head ache sometimes. And am much more sensitive to light as I only used red glasses when indoors. Since i did everything better and more optimal but developed issues, what would this be a sign of?

### Saichi — 2023-11-27T00:51:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sensitive-vs-no-sensitivity.28370/post-328694>

Becoming sensitive to nnemf is healthy.

My eyeballs hurt if I use my phone for more than 20 or so seconds.

### Brent Patrick — 2023-11-27T02:54:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sensitive-vs-no-sensitivity.28370/post-328696>

> [jeremiasbtome said:](/goto/post?id=328693)
>
> Before February I would use tech daily as the normal person does without turning on airplane mode or any safety precautions. I felt normal and had no aware health issues other than myopia and tourettes which were not bad.
> Then after february I began living more like a black swan mitochondriac, being outside as much as possible, using fire at night, emf precautions (airplane mode, etc). Now months later I have gotten more sun than any other year possible, am 19 years old. Ate more fish and meat than any other year. And changed environent to lower latitude and daily hot sun compared to being in a high latitude.
> Basically did as much right as possible. But ever since doing the lifestyle I became sensitive to tech, using laptop or phone, I feel the radiation and heart pain sometimes and head ache sometimes. And am much more sensitive to light as I only used red glasses when indoors. Since i did everything better and more optimal but developed issues, what would this be a sign of?

The same happens to me ,I believe this is a good thing as you are senstive to your surrounding environment.If I go into a large store selling entertainment goods TV'S etc I become somewhat dizzy and uneasy in myself.If i hold a mobile phone I get a ache in my hand this tells me to stop what I am doing.The fact that you feel this effect is a tell tale sign as a species we are not compatable with man made devices (nnEMF).

Having saturated your cells with DHA you are more sensitive to enviromental signals.

Look at this as a good thing you have another Innate tool to navigate towards optimal health.

[https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/brv.12747](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/brv.12747)

**"The high DHA content favours transition to the MII state and ultimately accelerates receptor activation and efficiency of signal transduction, requiring less photons for its activation and thus enabling, for example, vision at lower light levels (Soubias _et al_., [2015](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/brv.12747#brv12747-bib-0237))"

"Thus, the specific annular lipid composition, in particular high amounts of DHA, acts as catalyser, reducing the energetic barrier for the required structural changes during receptor activation and increases its kinetics, while reducing the required quantity of stimuli (e.g. photons) to trigger this process and increasing the efficiency of the receptor. Possible consequences for consumers include, for example, a direct correlation between dietary DHA and hunting success in low-light conditions (Bell _et al_., [1995](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/brv.12747#brv12747-bib-0017); Navarro _et al_., [1997](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/brv.12747#brv12747-bib-0171))."

"Metaphorically, DHA is like the lubricant of an engine, reducing the friction of cylinder movement and indirectly contributing to increased power output and enhanced performance. As will be discussed in the next sections, the greatest effect of DHA deficiency can be linked to receptors found in neuronal structures, such as the eye (rhodopsin) or the cerebral cortex and the cerebellum (serotonin and dopamine receptors), which are responsible for visual acuity or cognition, memory and behaviour, respectively. This provides a direct link between DHA and sensory and cognitive abilities."**

### JanSz — 2023-11-27T15:33:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sensitive-vs-no-sensitivity.28370/post-328702>

> [jeremiasbtome said:](/goto/post?id=328693)

> Before February I would use tech daily as the normal person does without turning on airplane mode or any safety precautions. I felt normal and had no aware health issues other than myopia and tourettes which were not bad.
> Then after february I began living more like a black swan mitochondriac, being outside as much as possible, using fire at night, emf precautions (airplane mode, etc). Now months later I have gotten more sun than any other year possible, am 19 years old. Ate more fish and meat than any other year. **And changed environent to lower latitude and daily hot sun compared to being in a high latitude.**
> Basically did as much right as possible. But ever since doing the lifestyle I became sensitive to tech, using laptop or phone, I feel the radiation and heart pain sometimes and head ache sometimes. And am much more sensitive to light as I only used red glasses when indoors. Since i did everything better and more optimal but developed issues, what would this be a sign of?

**And changed environment to lower latitude and daily hot sun compared to being in a high latitude.
\=============**
How long have you been on lower latitude?
How much difference in time zones between you old and new location.
Any significant changes in the length of the day between your old and new location?
\------------------------
I have made two great moves in my life.
1\. Moved from Poland to NJ USA and live there mostly at (practically) the same location since 1971.
Great change for better in the way I could breathe. So much cleaner air than in Poland.
In Poland I was close to having surgery on my sinuses, problems went away in USA.
2\. In 1979 to 1980 I have spent slightly over one year, say 13 months in Brazil, tropical location.
Itaorna, Brazil. So practically no change in time zones, no jet lags.
Living in Brazil was really nice for at least about 3 months.
Then I become having problems with something that at first, I not able to identify.
It turns out that I was seriously bothered by the equal night and day times.
I had a home at the beach, just across the road.
Wife developed some girlfriends, and they were spending serious amounts of time on the beach, no sunscreens, no problems.
We ate lot of seafood, mosty lobsters that over there had no claws, just edible tails.
The noon sunlight newer bothered me, but the short days did.

### VictorianGirl — 2025-07-21T01:49:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sensitive-vs-no-sensitivity.28370/post-356695>

I am experiencing this same effect. The more I decrease, the more sensitive I become. I believe your body loses its tolerance to bad things the more you distance yourself from them. The cleaner from chemicals my home environment is, the more sensitive I am to chemicals from other homes; the less I am around people who wear synthetic fragrances, the more I can smell them coming off people even when just walking by; the healthier my diet is, the less tolerant I am towards processed foods. This is entirely a normal and healthy process, though inconvenient because it makes us less compatible with the modern world and more of a pariah.

# Thread: Red light bulbs for lamps
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-bulbs-for-lamps.28371/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TMellody — 2023-11-27T15:08:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-bulbs-for-lamps.28371/post-328701>

Hi there. Which red light bulbs are we using in our lamps at night? I’m seeing lots of 25w incandescent bulbs but 25w is hardly bright enough. Are we to avoid red LED bulbs? Thx.

### Freebird — 2023-11-27T19:42:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-bulbs-for-lamps.28371/post-328706>

I'm using the red 25w incandescent bulbs. I personally don't like LED's. Don't like the way the "arch" in the spectrum goes with LED's. I prefer incandescent or halogen. Others may have additional insights.

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-27T21:33:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-bulbs-for-lamps.28371/post-328715>

My understanding:
#1 - no light
#2 - candle light / oil lamps
#3 - incandescent / quartz (they put out NIR as well)
#4 - LED w/out flicker (add NIR if possible)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-nnemf-sensitivity-recently-potassium-deficiency-maybe.28380/post-328807>

Does anyone have any insight as to why my sensitivity to nnemfs has gone ballistic recently. I am caring for ageing parents in their house. I moved out in the spring and returned two weeks ago. Ringing in ears is insane. Tried mitigating with different devices, spoke to building across the street but as yet not found a way to decrease. I can't leave but need to figure out what changed. Could decreased potassium due to diet create more susceptibility? Doing everything else- light blocking, EZ water, fish/protein, local veg, sun at dawn, during day/dusk but this is new... Any insight would be greatly appreciated.

### 8Phoenix — 2023-11-30T16:28:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increased-nnemf-sensitivity-recently-potassium-deficiency-maybe.28380/post-328808>

### JanSz — 2023-11-30T16:46:40+0200

### Leaf — 2023-11-30T20:37:46+0200

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=328810)
>
> How have you figured that it is nnemfs that bothers you?
> What are your specific complains on this new location?
>
> ///

When I first returned the HVAC system on the roof of a school across the street had a high pitch hum that was keeping me awake. I woke with ringing in my ears. They fixed it happily but the ringing continues. There is a tower associated with the OPP (Ontario Provincial Police) at a now defunct police station which is part of an elementary school. I have turned off all wifi etc at night and this is a new noise since March. It's my guess its the tower. I'm open to ideas.... I'm thinking either my sensitivity is greater since being full on Leptin oriented or they've increased the tower to 5G in the last few months. Either way I'm not sleeping. If it were a mineral/vitamin deficiency I would look into it but unsure where to start. I am chasing the tower owners to get it removed since police are no longer there but, that might be a pipe dream. I can try though.

### [A quantum theory for the irreplaceable role of docosahexaenoic acid in neural cell signalling throughout evolution - PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23206328/)

Six hundred million years ago, the fossil record displays the sudden appearance of intracellular detail and the 32 phyla. The "Cambrian Explosion" marks the onset of dominant aerobic life. Fossil intracellular structures are so similar to extant organisms that they were likely made with similar...

 pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

"This helps explain why the DC current of regeneration Dr. Robert O. Becker was found on the surface of water and proteins. It is not found deep within a cell. In nerves, [Becker found the DC current of regeneration was under the myelin layer](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0688069711/ref=as_li_qf_sp_asin_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=0688069711&linkCode=as2&tag=jackru-20) but outside the axon. **For this reason, peripheral neuropathy is a big sign of a poor redox potential. _So is degenerative disc disease_**_._ Tissues need a higher redox potential to regenerate. Protein synthesis is the most expensive cost of energy a cell has. This tells us about ubiquitination rates. When you constantly have to turn over protein, you are wasting huge stores of energy stored within your cells." JK

Anyone who has good iodine and iodide intake and assimilation from their diet, has an excellent DC current body wide, and their brain improves its function as sleep improves. Becker showed that this is the current, is the DC current that regenerates all tissues in humans in autophagy during sleep. **Neurogenesis and immunity are two of the most important systems to regenerate in humans because this is where evolution gave us our longevity within the primate tree.**JK

# Thread: vasodilators CO2 and nitric oxide NO
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vasodilators-co2-and-nitric-oxide-no.28405/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-12-09T15:12:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vasodilators-co2-and-nitric-oxide-no.28405/post-329024>

**vasodilators CO2 and nitric oxide NO**

Vasodilators are substances that cause the blood vessels to relax and widen, which lowers the blood pressure and improves blood flow.
CO2 and NO are two natural vasodilators that have different mechanisms of action.

**CO2 (carbon dioxide)** is a by-product of cellular respiration, which occurs in all living cells.
CO2 can diffuse into the blood vessels and bind to hemoglobin, the protein that carries oxygen in red blood cells.
This reduces the amount of oxygen that hemoglobin can bind, which causes the red blood cells to release more oxygen to the tissues.
This increases the oxygen delivery and reduces the oxygen demand of the tissues, which lowers the blood pressure and improves blood flow1.

**NO (nitric oxide)** is a gas molecule that is produced by various enzymes in different parts of the body, such as endothelial cells, smooth muscle cells, and platelets.
NO can diffuse into the smooth muscle cells of blood vessels and activate an enzyme called guanylate cyclase, which increases the production of cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP).
cGMP causes the relaxation of smooth muscle cells, leading to vasodilation2.
NO also has other effects on vascular function, such as suppressing platelet aggregation, inhibiting smooth muscle proliferation, and enhancing angiogenesis3.

Both CO2 and NO are potent vasodilators that can lower blood pressure and improve blood flow in various conditions.
However, they have different effects on different types of vascular beds.
CO2 mainly affects systemic arteries and veins,
while NO mainly affects pulmonary arteries and capillaries1.
Therefore, they have different indications for use in clinical settings.

CO2 is used as a treatment for hypercapnia (high levels of carbon dioxide in the blood), which can cause respiratory acidosis (low pH), coma, or death.
CO2 can also be used as a therapeutic agent for pulmonary hypertension (high blood pressure in the lungs), which can cause shortness of breath, chest pain, or heart failure.
CO2 can be administered by inhalation or injection1.

NO is used as a treatment for pulmonary hypertension (high blood pressure in the lungs), especially persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN), which is a life-threatening condition that affects some infants after birth.
NO can also be used as a diagnostic tool for pulmonary hypertension during right heart catheterization (a procedure that measures pressure in different chambers of the heart).
NO can be administered by inhalation using a device called an inhaler.

I hope this information helps you understand more about vasodilators CO2 and NO.

### JanSz — 2023-12-09T15:58:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vasodilators-co2-and-nitric-oxide-no.28405/post-329027>

When we are producing ketones, we are already in the stress state.
**ketones indicate stress state. That is why carbs should have priority.**

interested in the
**relationship between ketones, stress, and carbs.**
Ketones are molecules that are produced when your body breaks down fat for energy.
They can be used as an alternative fuel source by your brain and other organs when glucose (sugar) is scarce.
However, ketones can also have some negative effects on your health, especially **when they accumulate in high levels****.**
One of the possible effects of **ketones is that they can increase the production of stress hormones, such as cortisol and adrenaline.**
These hormones can help you cope with acute stress situations, such as a fight or flight response.
[**However, chronic or prolonged exposure to high levels of stress hormones can have detrimental consequences on your physical and mental well-being**](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/24003-ketosis)[**1**](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/24003-ketosis).
Another possible effect of ketones is that they can impair your glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity.
Insulin is a hormone that helps your cells take up glucose from the blood and use it for energy or storage.
When you eat a high-carb diet, your insulin level rises to facilitate glucose uptake.
When you eat a low-carb diet, such as the keto diet, your insulin level drops to prevent glucose from entering the cells.
[This can lead to a state of insulin resistance, which means that your cells become less responsive to insulin and require more of it to function properly](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/24003-ketosis)[2](https://peterattiamd.com/ketones-carbohydrates-can-co-exist/).
[Insulin resistance can contribute to various metabolic disorders, such as type 2 diabetes, obesity, cardiovascular disease, and fatty liver disease](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/24003-ketosis)[2](https://peterattiamd.com/ketones-carbohydrates-can-co-exist/).
[It can also affect your mood and cognitive function by altering the balance of neurotransmitters in your brain](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/24003-ketosis)[3](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-021-02185-0).
[Some studies have suggested that ketones may have some neuroprotective effects by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the brain](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/24003-ketosis)[4](https://perfectketo.com/how-many-carbs-on-keto/).
However, more research is needed to confirm this hypothesis and understand how ketones affect different aspects of brain function.
Therefore, it is not clear whether carbs should have priority over ketones in terms of health outcomes.
Some experts recommend limiting carbs on a keto diet to avoid excessive ketone production and potential side effects.
Others suggest that carbs can be beneficial for some people who need them for energy or performance reasons.
The best approach may depend on individual factors, such as goals, preferences, medical conditions, and lifestyle.

### JanSz — 2023-12-09T16:21:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vasodilators-co2-and-nitric-oxide-no.28405/post-329028>

**But when eating high carb diet we still are able to not eat high glycemic index carbs.**

I see that you are curious about how eating a high carb diet affects your blood sugar levels.
A high carb diet is one that provides more carbohydrates than your body needs for energy.
Carbohydrates are the main source of glucose, which is the fuel for your cells.
However, not all carbohydrates have the same effect on your blood sugar.
Some carbohydrates are digested quickly and cause a rapid rise in blood sugar, while others are digested slowly and cause a gradual rise in blood sugar.
The glycemic index (GI) is a measure of how quickly a carbohydrate raises your blood sugar compared to pure glucose.
[**Foods with a high GI (70 or higher)** cause a large spike in blood sugar, while foods with a low GI (55 or less) cause a small or no change in blood sugar](https://www.health.harvard.edu/healthbeat/a-good-guide-to-good-carbs-the-glycemic-index)[1](https://www.health.harvard.edu/healthbeat/a-good-guide-to-good-carbs-the-glycemic-index).
[The GI of a food depends on several factors, such as the type and amount of carbohydrate, the presence of fiber, fat, protein, and other nutrients, and the cooking method](https://www.diabeteshealth.com/high-carb-low-glycemic-diets-with-riva-greenberg/)[2](https://www.diabeteshealth.com/high-carb-low-glycemic-diets-with-riva-greenberg/).
Eating a high carb diet does not necessarily mean that you will have high blood sugar levels. It depends on what kind of carbs you eat and how much you eat.
If you eat mostly low GI foods, such as fruits, vegetables, whole grains, beans, nuts, and seeds, you can still enjoy them without worrying too much about your blood sugar levels.
[These foods provide fiber, vitamins, minerals, antioxidants, and phytochemicals that can help lower your risk of chronic diseases](https://www.health.harvard.edu/healthbeat/a-good-guide-to-good-carbs-the-glycemic-index)[3](https://www.hsph.harvard.edu/nutritionsource/carbohydrates/carbohydrates-and-blood-sugar/).
They also keep you feeling full and satisfied for longer.

However, if you eat mostly high GI foods, such as white bread, white rice, pasta, pastries, candy, soda, and alcohol, you may experience frequent spikes and crashes in your blood sugar levels.
[These can lead to symptoms such as hunger cravings, fatigue weakness mood swings irritability anxiety depression difficulty concentrating memory problems weight gain inflammation oxidative stress insulin resistance type 2 diabetes cardiovascular disease fatty liver disease](https://www.health.harvard.edu/healthbeat/a-good-guide-to-good-carbs-the-glycemic-index)[4](https://www.betterhealth.vic.gov.au/health/healthyliving/carbohydrates-and-the-glycaemic-index).
Therefore it is important to choose wisely when it comes to carbs on a high carb diet.
You should aim to eat more low GI foods and limit or avoid high GI foods as much as possible.
You should also balance your carbs with protein and healthy fats to slow down digestion and absorption of glucose.
[You should also monitor your portion sizes and track your carb intake using tools such as food labels nutrition databases apps or scales](https://thinlicious.com/glycaemic-index/)[5](https://thinlicious.com/glycaemic-index/).

### JanSz — 2023-12-09T16:31:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vasodilators-co2-and-nitric-oxide-no.28405/post-329029>

At any particular moment cells are able to burn carbs or fat primarily.
....

That is a very interesting question. The answer is not so simple, because it depends on many factors, such as the type of exercise, the intensity of the exercise, the amount and timing of food intake, and the individual’s metabolism. However, I will try to give you a general overview of how cells burn carbs or fat primarily.
Cells are able to burn both carbs and fat for energy, but they prefer to use one over the other depending on the availability and demand of each fuel source.
Carbs are stored in the blood as glucose (sugar) and in the muscles as glycogen (a form of starch).
Fat is stored in adipose tissue (fat cells) and can be released into the bloodstream when needed.
When you eat a meal that contains both carbs and fat, your body will first use up the carbs from your blood and glycogen stores.
This will provide energy for your brain and other organs that need glucose.
However, if you do not eat enough carbs or if you have a high-carb diet, your body may run out of glucose quickly and switch to burning fat for energy.
Fat is more efficient than carbs at producing ATP (the molecule that stores energy in cells), which means that your body can burn more fat per unit of oxygen than carbs.
However, fat also produces more ketones **(molecules that are toxic to some cells**) than carbs when burned.
Therefore, **burning fat requires more oxygen than burning carbs.**
When you do high-intensity exercise, such as sprinting or lifting weights, your body needs a lot of energy in a short time.
This means that it needs to use both carbs and fat quickly.
However, since your blood glucose level drops rapidly during high-intensity exercise, your body will prioritize using fat over carbs for fuel. This is because fat can provide more energy per gram than carbs.
However, this does not mean that you will burn more fat than carbs during high-intensity exercise. [In fact, most of the calories burned during high-intensity exercise come from both carbs and fats](https://www.self.com/story/the-myth-of-the-fat-burning-zone)[1](https://www.self.com/story/the-myth-of-the-fat-burning-zone).
[The proportion of each fuel source depends on how much glycogen you have left in your muscles after eating a meal before exercising](https://www.self.com/story/the-myth-of-the-fat-burning-zone)[2](https://learn.genetics.utah.edu/content/metabolism/bbs/).
[If you have enough glycogen stored in your muscles (about 2-3 grams per kilogram of body weight), you will burn mostly carbs during high-intensity exercise](https://www.self.com/story/the-myth-of-the-fat-burning-zone)[3](https://www.hprc-online.org/physical-fitness/training-performance/how-your-body-burns-fat-and-carbs-during-exercise).
[If you have little or no glycogen stored in your muscles (about 0-1 gram per kilogram of body weight), you will burn mostly fats during high-intensity exercise](https://www.self.com/story/the-myth-of-the-fat-burning-zone)[4](https://www.mskcc.org/news/cancer-cells-eat-fat-grow-and-spread).
Therefore, **at any particular moment cells are able to burn either carbs or fat** primarily depending on what kind of fuel source is available and what kind of demand is placed on them by physical activity. However, it is important to note that this does not mean that eating more or less carbs or fats will make you burn more or less calories during exercise. The total amount of calories burned depends on how much energy you expend regardless of what kind of fuel source you use.

### JanSz — 2023-12-09T16:53:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vasodilators-co2-and-nitric-oxide-no.28405/post-329030>

**In a healthy alethic person having low body fat, low blood glucose is causing stress in similar way as ketones are producing body stress.**

In a healthy, athletic person with low body fat, both low blood glucose and high levels of ketones can cause stress in the body, but they do so in different ways.

**Low Blood Glucose:** When blood glucose levels drop too low, a condition known as hypoglycemia, the body responds by releasing stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline. [These hormones stimulate the liver to produce more glucose and make the body’s cells more resistant to insulin, leading to increased blood glucose levels](https://dtc.ucsf.edu/types-of-diabetes/type2/understanding-type-2-diabetes/how-the-body-processes-sugar/blood-sugar-stress/)[1](https://dtc.ucsf.edu/types-of-diabetes/type2/understanding-type-2-diabetes/how-the-body-processes-sugar/blood-sugar-stress/).
[Symptoms of hypoglycemia can include **fatigue, sweating, dizziness, hunger, shakiness, heart palpitations, and mood changes**](https://www.healthline.com/health/hypoketotic-hypoglycemia)[2](https://www.healthline.com/health/hypoketotic-hypoglycemia).

**Ketones:** In contrast, ketones are produced when the body breaks down fat for energy, a process that happens when glucose is scarce, such as during fasting or strenuous exercise. This is common in people following a ketogenic diet or in athletes with low body fat.
[**While ketones are an efficient energy source, their production also leads to increased oxidative stress in the mitochondria**](https://dtc.ucsf.edu/types-of-diabetes/type2/understanding-type-2-diabetes/how-the-body-processes-sugar/blood-sugar-stress/)**[3](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-021-02185-0).**
[This stress triggers a protective response in the cells, helping them cope with the increased oxidative stress and lower energy availability](https://dtc.ucsf.edu/types-of-diabetes/type2/understanding-type-2-diabetes/how-the-body-processes-sugar/blood-sugar-stress/)[3](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-021-02185-0).

It’s important to note that while both low blood glucose and high ketone levels can cause stress responses in the body, these mechanisms are part of the body’s normal response to energy demands and are typically well-regulated in healthy individuals.

# Thread: Have not used sun screen a single time this year and never burned
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-not-used-sun-screen-a-single-time-this-year-and-never-burned.28409/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NikkiYork — 2023-12-11T02:29:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-not-used-sun-screen-a-single-time-this-year-and-never-burned.28409/post-329076>

… not even in the desert being out all day.

I kid you not.

Starting May I sat in the morning sun, almost daily between 30 minutes to 90 minutes. It got me a great tan.

All through the summer I never used any sunscreen and I didn’t worry about it. I went to the desert and stood in 100 degrees all day in a bikini. I did not burn.

I had gone to that same desert many times and always burned with sunscreen on.

Ergo: having a sun callus works and I was able to build it just by consciously getting the morning sun.

On top of that I feel like I am looking healthier than the past years. Two thumbs up to Jack’s strategy.

# Thread: US medical system is becoming ‘unrecognizable’
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-medical-system-is-becoming-%E2%80%98unrecognizable%E2%80%99.28417/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-12-13T18:26:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-medical-system-is-becoming-%E2%80%98unrecognizable%E2%80%99.28417/post-329184>

**Passing those health check$**
So, how not good is USA’s current medical system?
Per a very high-ranking doctor in charge of one of our major hospitals:

**“It’s become an unrecognizable system.
We fight for health while financial professionals are buying it up — and out.
Buying out the professionals.
Beth Israel? Shutting down.**
**Mt. Sinai losing fortunes.**
**NYC hospitals are going out of business.
The medical system has hit financial stress.**

“Hiring’s low. **Banks are running things.** Pre-pandemic Wall Street saw lots of waste. Now administering to America’s aged, infirmed and those in urgent need is private equity.

“Hedge funders — some old-money firms — are buying up MDs.
Paying doctors to sell their practices. Sums offered beat what they’d earn with stethoscopes.
This city is old and has big numbers of little walk-in curbside clinics.

“Even more than hundreds of them are being bought up. **Then re-sold.** [**To Walgreens.**](https://www.wsj.com/articles/walgreens-unit-close-to-roughly-9-billion-deal-with-summit-health-11667793542)
Walgreens so far paid out $9 _billion_.
**They bought every urologist in one state.**
All these medical professionals — in their 50s and 60s — retired immediately. They’re now shareholders.

“Young doctors have begun working for Walgreens.
NY state is now trying to commoditize health care.
Selling it like it’s pork belly.
They’re even picking off young medical professionals and leaving behind a vacuum.”

3 in 4 believe they know their own health better than their doctor does.

### JanSz — 2023-12-13T19:25:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-medical-system-is-becoming-%E2%80%98unrecognizable%E2%80%99.28417/post-329192>

Why Beth Israel is Shutting down. Mt. Sinai losing fortunes. NYC hospitals are going out of business.

There are several reasons why hospitals like Beth Israel and Mt. Sinai in New York City are facing financial difficulties:
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

1.  **Beth Israel**: The hospital, part of the Mount Sinai Medical Center, has been struggling financially for years. [It lost more than $1 billion over the past decade](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-10-30/nyc-s-beth-israel-hospital-to-shut-in-july-after-years-of-losses)[1](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-10-30/nyc-s-beth-israel-hospital-to-shut-in-july-after-years-of-losses). [The hospital’s census was only 28% full year-to-date through August, contributing to an “excessive burden” on the system](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-10-30/nyc-s-beth-israel-hospital-to-shut-in-july-after-years-of-losses)[1](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-10-30/nyc-s-beth-israel-hospital-to-shut-in-july-after-years-of-losses). [**As a result, Mount Sinai Medical Center decided to close the Beth Israel campus**](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-10-30/nyc-s-beth-israel-hospital-to-shut-in-july-after-years-of-losses)[**1**](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-10-30/nyc-s-beth-israel-hospital-to-shut-in-july-after-years-of-losses).

1.  **Mount Sinai**: The hospital is facing financial struggles due to a combination of factors. [One of the major issues is the shift to outpatient care, which has led to a decrease in inpatient volumes](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-10-30/nyc-s-beth-israel-hospital-to-shut-in-july-after-years-of-losses)[2](https://www.politico.com/newsletters/weekly-new-york-health-care/2023/02/06/contentious-mount-sinai-merger-plan-heads-for-a-vote-00081225). [Additionally, the hospital is dealing with higher labor and supply costs](https://www.politico.com/newsletters/weekly-new-york-health-care/2023/02/06/contentious-mount-sinai-merger-plan-heads-for-a-vote-00081225)[2](https://www.politico.com/newsletters/weekly-new-york-health-care/2023/02/06/contentious-mount-sinai-merger-plan-heads-for-a-vote-00081225). [Mount Sinai is also planning a merger with the New York Eye and Ear Infirmary, which is expected to help improve its financial position](https://www.politico.com/newsletters/weekly-new-york-health-care/2023/02/06/contentious-mount-sinai-merger-plan-heads-for-a-vote-00081225)[2](https://www.politico.com/newsletters/weekly-new-york-health-care/2023/02/06/contentious-mount-sinai-merger-plan-heads-for-a-vote-00081225).

1.  **NYC Hospitals**: Many hospitals in New York City and across the country are facing similar challenges. [The COVID-19 pandemic has exacerbated these issues, leading to increased costs and decreased revenues](https://www.statnews.com/2020/05/26/hospitals-are-busier-than-ever-and-going-out-of-business/)[3](https://www.statnews.com/2020/05/26/hospitals-are-busier-than-ever-and-going-out-of-business/)[4](https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/factcheck/2021/09/24/fact-check-hospitals-nationwide-both-overrun-and-understaffed/5700211001/). [Staffing shortages and burnout are also significant problems, with many healthcare workers leaving the profession due to the physical and mental toll](https://www.cnn.com/2023/01/11/business/nurses-strike-staff-shortage-problem/index.html)[5](https://www.cnn.com/2023/01/11/business/nurses-strike-staff-shortage-problem/index.html)[6](https://www.cnn.com/2023/01/09/business/nyc-nurses-strike/index.html)[4](https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/factcheck/2021/09/24/fact-check-hospitals-nationwide-both-overrun-and-understaffed/5700211001/).

These are complex issues with no easy solutions. [It’s important to note that while some hospitals are closing or facing financial difficulties, others are adapting and finding ways to continue providing care to their communities](https://www.beckershospitalreview.com/finance/hospital-closures-bankruptcies-expected-to-spike-in-2023.html)[7](https://www.beckershospitalreview.com/finance/hospital-closures-bankruptcies-expected-to-spike-in-2023.html). For the most accurate and up-to-date information, it would be best to contact the hospitals directly or refer to official announcements and news reports.

//////

### JanSz — 2023-12-13T19:29:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-medical-system-is-becoming-%E2%80%98unrecognizable%E2%80%99.28417/post-329193>

What is the financial status of Sloan Kettering Memorial

Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC) is one of the leading cancer centers in the world. Here’s a summary of its recent financial status:

-   [**Operating Revenues**: In 2020, MSKCC reported total operating revenues of $5,407,196,000](https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/node/223396/documents/msk_ar2021_financialsummary.pdf)[1](https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/node/223396/documents/msk_ar2021_financialsummary.pdf).
-   [**Operating Expenses**: In the same year, total operating expenses were $5,824,368,000](https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/node/223396/documents/msk_ar2021_financialsummary.pdf)[1](https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/node/223396/documents/msk_ar2021_financialsummary.pdf).
-   [**Net Assets**: MSKCC reported a decrease in net assets without donor restrictions of $246 million in the first quarter of 2022](https://www.beckershospitalreview.com/finance/memorial-sloan-kettering-reports-operating-loss-of-13-5-million.html)[2](https://www.beckershospitalreview.com/finance/memorial-sloan-kettering-reports-operating-loss-of-13-5-million.html).
-   [**Operating Loss**: **The center saw a swing from a $235.1 million** **operating gain in 2021 to a 2022 operating loss of $248.1** **million**](https://www.beckershospitalreview.com/finance/memorial-sloan-kettering-reports-483m-slide-in-operating-income.html)**[3](https://www.beckershospitalreview.com/finance/memorial-sloan-kettering-reports-483m-slide-in-operating-income.html).**
-   [**Cash and Investments**: MSKCC’s financial profile is strong with over $8 billion of unrestricted cash and investments](https://www.fitchratings.com/research/us-public-finance/fitch-affirms-memorial-sloan-kettering-cancer-center-ny-revenue-bonds-at-aa-outlook-stable-15-04-2022)[4](https://www.fitchratings.com/research/us-public-finance/fitch-affirms-memorial-sloan-kettering-cancer-center-ny-revenue-bonds-at-aa-outlook-stable-15-04-2022).

[Please note that these figures are subject to change and for the most accurate and up-to-date information, it would be best to refer to the official financial reports of MSKCC](https://www.mskcc.org/public-notices/financial-information)[5](https://www.mskcc.org/public-notices/financial-information).

### Gary Hamer — 2023-12-15T15:27:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-in-el-salvador.28421/post-329252>

Hey all, I had seen a post about someone recommending a lab to use while in El Salvador but I cannot find the thread searching for it.

I found a few labs in ES through Google that could work but wanted to hear some recommendations.

We inquired about a few different labs down where we are in Costa Rica and these were the prices we got:

25-hydroxyvitamin D test: $90 USD

Omega 6:3 serum test: not available

HS-CRP: $25

TSH level: $36
Free T4:$36
Free T3: $36
Reverse T3 : not available

Leptin: not available
HDL: $45
Ferritin: $35
DHEA: $35

# Thread: CT and extreme shivering
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Goswin de Boer — 2023-12-15T16:07:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-329253>

I've searched the forum for posts about shivering, but I still have some uncertainty. So please allow me...

I'm doing cold showers every morning for the last three years already. Since october I added the cold plunge in a tub in our garden. I submerge myself up to the chin, hands submerged as well, and try to let the cold in. And I can ... sort of.

I've been able to handle 5-6 mins @ 32F. Lately the water is at 45F, and I manage 30 minutes of that. No problems with my breathing at all, some shivering gradually building up, but I can still turn the shivers off for a while. They always come back though.

But then, when I step out and get dressed, my shivers get almost out of control. I can hardly walk straight, and for the next 45 mins to an hour I just shake like mad. And then it gradually stops. I read a meal before CT is recommended, so I will do that tomorrow. But I'm uncertain about the severity of the shivers.

My questions are:
\- are the shivers here to stay, or do they gradually go away?
(I didn't have them as much, building up to 30 mins)
\- do shivers signal I'm not adapted all that well, or can they be a good sign?
\- are all shivers the same, or do my severe shivers signal something else than normal shivers (like I had coming out of the pool as a kid)?

Thanks for helping out.

### Goswin de Boer — 2023-12-19T15:56:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-329358>

Little update: a meal beforehand does help a little. The shivers are still severe, and I can't hold them off once I get out of the tub. But I can at least walk normally and have a decent conversation. So that's a little progress

The more body fat the less shivering.... in your case the less body fat the more shivering.

Have you increased your DHA ?

Preloading your semiconductors with DHA allows for increased cell signalling.

This implies that The colder it is the more omega 3’s are need for cell membrane signalling.

This is also why northern cold waters are saturated with Fish that are high in Omega 3's ,this Is not the case with warm water fish around the equator.

DHA becomes magnetically drawn to tissues with high mitochondrial densities which are magnetic.

The more electrons in cell membranes the better you handle cold.

DHA facilitates this.

### kris90 — 2023-12-21T22:02:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-329392>

> [Goswin de Boer said:](/goto/post?id=329388)
>
> And would that also go for us? That would mean that we 'd also have to consume omega-6 prior to becoming cold adapted... Correct my reasoning when / where faulty.

Yes and no. I believe his point is that when a mammal's inflammation levels peak (which is usually in Fall), they are in that state of being ready to "den", (i.e. inflammation causes fatigue and "low energy"), at which point all the reversal (i.e. benefits of CT) of said inflammation begins once they enter hibernation (i.e. sleep, fasting/ketosis, cold/grounding).

Since we don't hibernate, we should never purposely get ourselves into an inflammatory state. Just cut carbs out in the Fall, and begin embracing CT while fueling on a ketogenic diet. I think the point he makes is that mammals use this pathway to reverse their self-induced inflammatory state, and humans can do the same thing to reverse ours which is self-induced but not for our own good (i.e. blue light, EMFs, circadian mismatches, etc).

### coho3131 — 2023-12-31T02:07:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-329662>

It depends on the temperature of the water and how much free time I have.

Today in a local farm pond the water temperature was 40 degrees today and I did about 3 minutes in the water in shorts only, then walked home in 38 degree air temperature (28 degreee windchill) without drying off for 10 minutes in only shorts and sandals, then stayed outside for about 10 minutes more to dry off by evaporation. I came inside and started to feel at baseline about 15 minutes later. I did not take a shower until much later after doing some outdoor chores in shorts only.

In the Fall when the water was in the 50s I would stay in the water much longer and even swim a little bit.

### Saichi — 2023-12-31T02:10:05+0200

### coho3131 — 2023-12-31T02:16:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-329664>

I see your point!
I don't think 30+ min through the ice like today would be good for anyone.
The lake had thin ice, but the water temperature measured 40 F on a cheap pool thermometer.
In a static column of natural water the nadir is 39.2 F.
I will try to increase the time though.
Right now my toes start to hurt after a few minutes, and I don't think it is natural to wear neoprene socks.

### Saichi — 2023-12-31T03:23:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-329668>

> I will try to increase the time though.
> Right now my toes start to hurt after a few minutes, and I don't think it is natural to wear neoprene socks.

I wear neoprene gloves and booties when I do CT in city banyas. I don't wear them when I'm doing ocean CT since swimming keeps my fingers and toes warm but I do wear a neoprene cap to prevent surfer's ear when big waves take me under. Years ago I used to do mild banya CT of 52 deg and I didn't need gloves or booties to stay comfortable. I always wear gloves booties indoors now but I'm doing 35 to 50 minutes in 43 to 48 deg sometimes twice a day.

Very nice 43-48 F times! Twice a day also impressive!

### coho3131 — 2023-12-31T17:06:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-329681>

Last winter I wore neoprene socks and gloves when I needed to go through the ice on the pond.

This year I decided that if I needed artificial protection from the cold to prevent pain, then that water was too cold for my health.

So, now I just stay in until the pain gets significant, and then I get out.

Going barefoot on the mud/sand/rock bottom is actually less cold for me than wearing sandals because the bottom of my foot sinks in a little and gets insulated.

Paced breathing and visualization also have delayed the onset of pain.

### kris90 — 2024-01-03T17:13:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-329769>

Neoprene gloves and footwear are game changers for sure. I'm doing anywhere's from 3-10 mins daily in 30-32 F water now and seems to be more than enough. 10 mins works best for a good shiver if I let myself evaporate and stay outside for a few mins. Interestingly, I can immediately halt the shivering if I follow breathing pattern: 5 second deep nasal inhale, hold for a couple seconds, and 10-15 second nasal exhale, hold for a couple seconds and repeat.
