### mvivier — 2024-01-30T00:12:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-330724>

[Andrew Huberman, a neuroscientist and tenured professor at Stanford University, has discussed the benefits of **deliberate cold exposure** in his podcast](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance)[1](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance). [He explains that shivering is a natural response to cold exposure and can have a significant impact on fat loss](https://www.hubermanlab.readablepods.com/blog/fat-loss-and-cold-exposure/)[2](https://www.hubermanlab.readablepods.com/blog/fat-loss-and-cold-exposure/). [Shivering and small movements can trigger the release of epinephrine and norepinephrine in the brain and body, which can make us feel alert and increase our level of energy and focus](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance)[1](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance).

In addition, Dr. [Huberman suggests that deliberate cold exposure can help build resilience and grit by forcing oneself to embrace the stress of cold exposure as a meaningful self-directed challenge](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance)[1](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance). Not sure if this thought has been approved by Dr. Jack. Does anyone have a link to the "Two Vikings" podcast he references?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-330746>

> [mvivier said:](/goto/post?id=330724)
>
> [Andrew Huberman, a neuroscientist and tenured professor at Stanford University, has discussed the benefits of **deliberate cold exposure** in his podcast](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance)[1](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance). [He explains that shivering is a natural response to cold exposure and can have a significant impact on fat loss](https://www.hubermanlab.readablepods.com/blog/fat-loss-and-cold-exposure/)[2](https://www.hubermanlab.readablepods.com/blog/fat-loss-and-cold-exposure/). [Shivering and small movements can trigger the release of epinephrine and norepinephrine in the brain and body, which can make us feel alert and increase our level of energy and focus](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance)[1](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance).
>
> In addition, Dr. [Huberman suggests that deliberate cold exposure can help build resilience and grit by forcing oneself to embrace the stress of cold exposure as a meaningful self-directed challenge](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance)[1](https://www.hubermanlab.com/newsletter/the-science-and-use-of-cold-exposure-for-health-and-performance). Not sure if this thought has been approved by Dr. Jack. Does anyone have a link to the "Two Vikings" podcast he references?

I would suggest reading the CT blogs.

### Donald Shrump Jr. — 2024-06-20T14:12:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-extreme-shivering.28422/post-335928>

I had the same issue for about 2 weeks then your muscle begin unnoticeable shivering while in the water (which is why you can last 30+ min), once I was adapted the “Rebounds” decreased as I focused on being in ketosis. The Ketosis got rid of my uncontrollable rebounds. I “listen to my shiver” based on all the variables in life I don’t push the clock, just the feel. I deep CT all winter dropped a ton of weight, increased redox, etc. Keep it going!!

### [Have water blisters from sunburn? Here is everything you need to know on how to treat and prevent small sunburn blisters - Burn and Reconstructive Centers of America](https://burncenters.com/community/have-water-blisters-from-sunburn-here-is-everything-you-need-to-know-on-how-to-treat-and-prevent-small-sunburn-blisters/)

Blisters, also known as bullae, are pockets of fluid between the epidermis (top layer of skin) and the dermis (second layer of skin). There are two common types of blisters people are likely to experience: blood blisters and “water” blisters. Blood blisters are pockets of blood and fluids that...

 burncenters.com

Is there any topology to understand? Sometimes our eyes become full of tears from sun. A smart man I know said that this is our system trying to absorb light. Do drops on my skin have a similar effect?

I have noticed that some of these drops pull back into the skin, some I crack (I try not to) , while others break on their own. (I've always thought bubbles like that were third-degree burns. What are they?

### JanSz — 2023-12-27T02:10:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pentadecanoic-acid-c15-0-an-essential-fatty-acid-leading-longevity-enhancing-compounds.28447/post-329500>

### [Pentadecanoic Acid (C15:0), an Essential Fatty Acid, Shares Clinically Relevant Cell-Based Activities with Leading Longevity-Enhancing Compounds](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-6643/15/21/4607)

Pentadecanoic acid (C15:0) is an essential odd-chain saturated fatty acid with broad activities relevant to protecting cardiometabolic, immune, and liver health. C15:0 activates AMPK and inhibits mTOR, both of which are core components of the human longevity pathway. To assess the potential for...

 www.mdpi.com

Pentadecanoic Acid (C15:0), an Essential Fatty Acid, Shares Clinically Relevant Cell-Based Activities with Leading Longevity-Enhancing Compounds

Published: 30 October 2023

C15:0 activates AMPK and inhibits mTOR, both of which are core components of the human longevity pathway.

\=============
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Keywords: C15:0; pentadecanoic acid; rapamycin; metformin; acarbose; longevity; essential fatty acid

“geroscience hypothesis”

Although metformin has been used as a medicine for nearly four centuries, it has been more recently evaluated as a candidate longevity-enhancing compound \[61\].

we compared the primary human cell phenotypic profile of C15:0 with acarbose, metformin, and rapamycin

Of the four compounds tested, pure C15:0 (FA15) had the most dose-dependent annotated activities

All four compounds (C15:0, rapamycin, metformin, and acarbose) were found to be non-cytotoxic at all concentrations

**At their optimal doses, C15:0 shared numerous clinically relevant activities with rapamycin and metformin.**

### JanSz — 2023-12-27T02:28:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pentadecanoic-acid-c15-0-an-essential-fatty-acid-leading-longevity-enhancing-compounds.28447/post-329502>

[Pentadecanoic acid (15:0) is a straight-chain saturated fatty acid that is found in foods such as **beef products, dairy and egg products, lamb, veal, game products, sausages and luncheon meats, American Indian/Alaska Native foods, restaurant foods, and fast foods**](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
[Pentadecanoic acid has several health benefits, including anti-inflammatory properties and the ability to regulate blood sugar levels](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170)[2](https://www.consumerlab.com/answers/what-is-pentadecanoic-acid-and-does-it-have-health-benefits/pentadecanoic-acid/).
If you’re looking to incorporate more pentadecanoic acid into your diet, here are some foods that are rich in this nutrient:

1.  [**Whipped Butter (With Salt)**: 801 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
2.  [**Sunflower Oil (High Oleic, 70% & Over)**: 800 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
3.  [**Butter (Salted)**: 753 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
4.  [**Raw Fresh Lamb (Separable Fat, Australian Imported)**: 470 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
5.  [**Cooked Fresh Lamb (Separable Fat, Australian Imported)**: 464 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
6.  [**Cooked Fresh Lamb Seam Fat (Australian Imported)**: 406 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
7.  [**Raw Veal (Separable Fat, Australian)**: 393 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
8.  [**Cooked Fresh Lamb (Australian Imported, External Fat)**: 369 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
9.  [**Raw Seam Fat (Grass-Fed Beef, Australian Imported)**: 362 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).
10.  [**Heavy Whipping Cream (Fluid)**: 353 mg per 100g](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170) [1](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170).

[Please note that the above list is not exhaustive](https://www.careomnia.com/nutrition-tool-nutrient?nutrientID=170)

# Thread: Cat allergy and how I dealt with it.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cat-allergy-and-how-i-dealt-with-it.28453/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lukassss — 2023-12-28T11:19:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cat-allergy-and-how-i-dealt-with-it.28453/post-329536>

I spent my entire childhood with cats. There were 6 cats in my family home.
When I left my family home 20 years ago, returning to my family home and any other place where cats live became a problem for me.

Staying in rooms where there were cats or petting a cat caused me: initially, a severe runny nose, watery and itchy eyes. After spending the night in such a house, I felt very bad.

That's why for me every Christmas and Easter are 4 days of fighting for life...

I have tested many solutions to this problem.
\- antihistamines worked only in the initial phase. I did NOT take medications, I did not consider them to be a solution.
\- I tested various supplements in high doses - 0 effects
\- CT gave an amazing effect. Having a stuffy nose, runny nose and watery eyes, within 30 seconds of entering the icy water my nose was clear and my allergy symptoms disappeared. This method has helped me for the last 3 years. It had a short duration of action, like antihistamines.

This year for Christmas I fasted for 6 days (I didn't eat anything, only water).

**FASTING EFFECT**
I slept for 3 nights in a room with a cat, I could pet it. I had no allergy symptoms.
I will add that I started fasting 2 days before arriving at the house where there were cats. After 2 days I was already in ketosis.

Christmas without food and without allergies

### JanSz — 2023-12-28T14:58:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cat-allergy-and-how-i-dealt-with-it.28453/post-329539>

# Thread: Sperti use in winter outdoors
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sperti-use-in-winter-outdoors.28456/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kendall Toerner — 2023-12-29T01:06:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sperti-use-in-winter-outdoors.28456/post-329562>

I purchased a Sperti UV lamp to help with winter in Colorado Springs, and was going to purchase a Firedragon by EMR-TEK to balance the UV with IR.

It then occurred to me to just use the Sperti outside, getting CT as an added benefit.

As this is quite the environmental mismatch (but a fun variation of Sherpa quantum effects), and albeit necessary for my surfaces, does anyone know if the IR during winter at such a latitude is able to balance such strong UV? Especially on cloudy days with indirect sunlight. I'm basically thinking of using just the sun's IR with the Sperti, or adding in a Firedragon to also use outdoors alongside the sun.

My intuition as a photographer is that the sun is so bright, and half of it is IR light, so the sun should be enough. Curious to hear any habits of others supplementing with UV during winter. Not an option to travel or live other places during this next year of my life unfortunately.

### Marko Pollo — 2023-12-29T03:30:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sperti-use-in-winter-outdoors.28456/post-329566>

> [Kendall Toerner said:](/goto/post?id=329562)
>
> I purchased a Sperti UV lamp to help with winter in Colorado Springs, and was going to purchase a Firedragon by EMR-TEK to balance the UV with IR.
>
> It then occurred to me to just use the Sperti outside, getting CT as an added benefit.
>
> As this is quite the environmental mismatch (but a fun variation of Sherpa quantum effects), and albeit necessary for my surfaces, does anyone know if the IR during winter at such a latitude is able to balance such strong UV? Especially on cloudy days with indirect sunlight. I'm basically thinking of using just the sun's IR with the Sperti, or adding in a Firedragon to also use outdoors alongside the sun.
>
> My intuition as a photographer is that the sun is so bright, and half of it is IR light, so the sun should be enough. Curious to hear any habits of others supplementing with UV during winter. Not an option to travel or live other places during this next year of my life unfortunately.

Hey Kendall, Makes sense to me that outside would make more sense. my curiosity was piqued by your being a photographer. Can you use a digital camera as a spectrograph? I remember having a pictograph of the light for each exposure but to be honest i never thought much about it without my current interest. Can you take a pic and find out the intensity/ratios of different spectrums? Thanks

### Kendall Toerner — 2023-12-29T16:36:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sperti-use-in-winter-outdoors.28456/post-329579>

> [Marko Pollo said:](/goto/post?id=329566)
>
> Hey Kendall, Makes sense to me that outside would make more sense. my curiosity was piqued by your being a photographer. Can you use a digital camera as a spectrograph? I remember having a pictograph of the light for each exposure but to be honest i never thought much about it without my current interest. Can you take a pic and find out the intensity/ratios of different spectrums? Thanks

Hey Marko, very interesting idea with the DSLR. The histogram is primarily for measuring brightness levels of a color throughout the photo. Unfortunately these don't correspond to wavelengths, and it's likely even if they did, several filters inside the camera would need to be removed to get a proper reading beyond the visible spectrum. I'm unsure if consumer camera lenses would also negatively affect the accuracy of the wavelengths output.

I'm curious about finding a good spectrograph to test my environment as well. If you come across one to rent or one that's fairly inexpensive to buy with decent results let me know.

### Marko Pollo — 2023-12-29T18:21:12+0200

> µW vs mW (factor of 1000) . . . /cm2 vs /m2 (factor of 10,000)

Yes but mW is larger than µW. His device is measuring in µW/microwatts which is 1000x less than mW/milliwatts. UV in tropics is 375 milliwatts, not even remotely close to the 31 microwatts he measured (0.031 milliwatts)

Tropics UV compared to his measurement is 10,000x higher (albeit a mix of UVA and UVB, with UVB around 5%). That still leaves it at 500x higher UVB if his is an instantaneous measurement and not a measurement over time.
